urn:contentItem:6JHK-BKC3-S0RF-C55B-00000-00Federal Energy Regulatory Commission: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation submits tariff filing per 35.13(a)(2)(iii: 2026-05-05 Plum Creek E&P - 793 - 0.0.0 to be effective 4/28/2026 under ER26-2461 Filing Type : 102026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisFederal Energy Regulatory Commission: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation submits tariff filing per 35.13(a)(2)(iii: 2026-05-05 Plum Creek E&P - 793 - 0.0.0 to be effective 4/28/2026 under ER26-2461 Filing Type : 10

Washington: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation has submitted the following document to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission:

The Honorable Debbie-Anne A. ReeseSecretaryFederal Energy Regulatory Commission888 First Street, NEWashington, DC 20426RE: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporationRevised Engineering & Procurement Agreement for MISO Project J2104Service Agreement No. 793-NSPDocket No. ER26-____-000Dear Secretary Reese:Pursuant to Section 205 of the Federal Power Act, 16 U.S.C § 824d (2018), and Section 35.13 of the regulations of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“Commission”), 18 C.F.R § 35. 13 (2026), Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation (“NSPM”), hereby submits for filing the Revised Engineering and Procurement Agreement for MISO Project J2104 (“Revised Agreement”) between NSPM, Geronimo Power, LLC (“Geronimo”), and Midcontinent Independent System Operator, Inc. (“MISO”). Individually, NSPM, Geronimo, and MISO are referred to herein as “Party” and collectively as the “Parties”. The Agreement is submitted in accordance with Order No. 7141 and the Commission ’ s eTariff filing requirements.1. BackgroundNSPM and its utility operating company affiliate Northern States Power Company, a Wisconsin corporation (together the “NSP Companies”) are wholly owned utility operating company subsidiaries of Xcel Energy Inc., and are engaged in the business of generating, transmitting, distributing and selling electric power and energy and related services in the States of Minnesota, Michigan, North Dakota, South Dakota, and Wisconsin. The NSP Companies are transmission-owning members of the Midcontinent Independent System Operator, Inc. (“MISO”) and provide most transmission service pursuant to the MISO Open Access Transmission, Energy and Operating Reserve Markets Tariff on file with and accepted by the Commission. However, certain services are, or have been, provided pursuant to individual grandfathered agreements or the Xcel Energy Operating Companies Open Access Transmission Tariff (“Xcel Energy OATT”) on file with and accepted by the Commission.2 Individual NSPM service agreements are on file with the Commission in NSPM ’ s FERC Electric Tariff Second Revised Volume No. 3.1 Electronic Tariff Filings, Order No. 714, 124 FERC ¶ 61,270 (2008); order on clarification, Order No. 714-A, 147 FERC ¶ 61,115 (2014).2 The Xcel Energy OATT was restated in eTariff and accepted for filing in Public Service Company of Colorado, Docket No. ER16-1422-000, unpublished letter order (August 16, 2016).The Honorable Debbie-Anne A. ReeseMay 5, 2026Page 2 of 6Geronimo is a wholly owned subsidiary of National Grid Renewables, a leading renewable energy company with a nationwide development portfolio. Under National Grid Renewables ’ ownership, Geronimo has continued its core focus on project origination and early-stage development while leveraging broader construction, financing, and asset management capabilities.On April 28, 2026, the Parties executed the Revised Agreement modifying certain provisions of the Engineering and Procurement Agreement for MISO Project J2104 (Original Agreement). The Original Agreement was executed as a conforming agreement under Attachment W-1 of the Xcel Energy OATT, effective June 20, 2025.2. The Instant FilingThe modifications to the Original Agreement proposed herein reflect necessary changes that occurred following its execution, including an updated in-service date, an extension of the termination provision in absence of a Generator Interconnection Agreement, and an increase to the security amount associated with upgrades required to accommodate MISO Project J2104. In addition, the Revised Agreement includes minor administrative modifications that may be considered non-conforming. Accordingly, the Revised Agreement is being filed out of an abundance of caution.Section 14 and Appendix A identify the minor modifications that NSPM deems to be non-conforming. Consistent with filing requirements, NSPM submits the Revised Agreement in both redline and clean formats.3. Proposed Effective Date and Request for Waiver of NoticeNSPM respectfully requests that the Commission grant waiver of its sixty (60) day prior notice requirement3 and allow the Revised Agreement to become effective on April 28, 2026, the date of execution. NSPM submits that good cause exists to grant the requested waiver. It is the Commission ’ s policy to allow waiver of the prior notice requirements for service agreements under a tariff filed within thirty (30) days of the requested effective date.4 Here, the Revised Agreement is being filed within 7 days of its requested effective date of April 28, 2026. Moreover, the Parties support an April 28, 2026, effective date for the Revised Agreement. NSPM requests April 28, 2026, to provide certainty to the Parties as to the status of the Revised Agreement.4. Description of Documents Being Submitted with Filing5a. This transmittal letter,b. A clean version of Service Agreement No. 793-NSP, andc. A redline version of Service Agreement No. 793-NSP.3 18 C.F.R § 35.3(a) (2026).4 Prior Notice and Filing Requirements under Part II of the Federal Power Act, 64 FERC ¶ 61,139 at 61,984 (1993). See also PJM Interconnection, LLC, 194 FERC ¶ 61,096 at P 14 (2026).5 The Original Agreement was reported on the EQR as 778-NSP. This eTariff filing designates the Revised Agreement as 793-NSP.The Honorable Debbie-Anne A. ReeseMay 5, 2026Page 3 of 65. Communications and CorrespondenceCommunications and correspondence with respect to this filing should be directed to each of the following: 6Service and PostingAn electronic version or notice of this filing will be served upon each of the state commissions having jurisdiction over NSPM and on representatives of the Parties. A courtesy copy will be provided to the Division of Electric Power Regulation (Central). In addition, a copy of this filing will be posted at the offices of NSPM at 414 Nicollet Mall in Minneapolis, Minnesota.Please direct any questions regarding this filing to Elizabeth Walkup at (612) 216-8223.Respectfully submitted,/s/ Jonathan EserJonathan EserAssistant General Counsel & Senior Director, Transmission Business RelationsXcel Energy Services Inc.On behalf ofNorthern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation6 NSPM requests waiver of Section 385.203(b)(3) of the Commission ’ s regulations to permit the designation of more than two persons upon whom service is to be made in this proceeding. 18 C.F.R § 385.203(b)(3) (2026).Jonathan EserAssistant General Counsel & Senior Director, Transmission Business RelationsXcel Energy Services Inc.701 Pennsylvania Ave. NW Suite 250Washington, DC 20004Email: Jonathan.Eser@xcelenergy.comMichèle B. BrüningPrincipal Transmission RepresentativeXcel Energy Services Inc.414 Nicollet Mall, 414-6Minneapolis, MN 55401Phone: (612) 330-5836Email: Michele.B.Bruning@xcelenergy.comJoseph W. LowellAssistant General CounselXcel Energy Services Inc.701 Pennsylvania Ave. NW Suite 250Washington, DC 20004Phone: (202) 661-4491Email: Joe.W.Lowell@xcelenergy.comSERVICE LISTMs. Natalie Tingle-StewartDirectorDivision of Electric Power Regulation (Central)Federal Energy Regulatory Commission888 First Street, NEWashington, DC 20426Email: natalie.tingle-stewart@ferc.govMr Mike BullActing Executive SecretaryMinnesota Public Utilities Commission121 7th Place E., Suite 350Saint Paul, MN 55101-2147Email: mike.bull@state.mn.usMs Patricia Van GerpenExecutive DirectorSouth Dakota Public Utilities Commission500 E. Capitol Ave.Pierre, SD 57501-5070Email: patty.vangerpen@state.sd.usMr Steve KahlExecutive SecretaryNorth Dakota Public Service Commission600 E Boulevard, Dept. 408Bismarck, ND 58505-0480Email: pscexecutivesec@nd.govChris AyikaInterconnection ManagerGeronimo Power8400 Normandale Lake Boulevard, Suite 1200Bloomington, MN 55437Email: cayika@geronimopower.com952-641-4031Kalen ColemanMidcontinent Independent System Operator, Inc.720 City Center Dr.Carmel, IN 46032Telephone: (317) 219-8481kcoleman@misoenergy.orgagreements@misoenergy.orgCERTIFICATE OF SERVICEI, Elizabeth Walkup, hereby certify that I have on this day served a notice of the enclosed filing via electronic mail on each party designated on the attached Service List.Dated at Minneapolis, Minnesota this 5th day of May 2026./s/ Elizabeth WalkupTransmission Account Rep and Technical SpecialistXcel Energy Services Inc.414 Nicollet Mall, 414-6Minneapolis, MN 55401 Phone: (612) 216-8223Email: elizabeth.a.walkup@xcelenergy.comA

urn:contentItem:6JK4-2GS3-S92X-93SC-00000-00DTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) continues to outperform Duke Energy Corporation by 0.9% in the past month2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company (NYSE:DTE) continues to outperform Duke Energy Corporation by 0.9% in the past month

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's (NYSE:DTE) share price is continuing to outperform that of Duke Energy Corporation (NYSE:DUK). DTE Energy Company's return over the past month has been -5.1% and Duke Energy Corporation's return has been -6.0% over the same period.

Here is how DTE Energy Company and Duke Energy Corporation compare:

Company Name1 Month %3 Month%YTD %1 yr %DTE Energy Company-5.1-1.311.010.5Duke Energy Corporation-6.0-3.46.613.5

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

value.Title: House votes to nix Michigan 's 100% clean energy goal, but it's DOA in Senate

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJX-3W03-S0VH-V40S-00000-00House votes to nix Michigan's 100% clean energy goal, but it's DOA in Senate2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisMICHIGAN GOVERNMENTHouse votes to nix Michigan's 100% clean energy goal, but it's DOA in SenateJordyn Hermani

* The Michigan House on Tuesday voted mostly along party lines to roll back recently enacted statewide clean energy standards

* Republicans behind the push say the move will save ratepayers money on their energy bills, though Democrats are dubious

* Senate Majority Leader Winnie Brinks, D-Grand Rapids, on Tuesday said the two-bill package is DOA in the upper chamber

------------

LANSING Michigan House Republicans on Tuesday voted to repeal the state's recently enacted 100% clean energy standard, a move the state Senate's top lawmaker says stands "no chance" of getting a vote in that chamber.

House lawmakers passed House Bill 5710 in a 58-47 vote, while companion bill HB 5711 passed 57-48. State Rep. Peter Herzberg, of Westland, was the lone Democrat to vote with Republicans on the first bill.

The package would roll back legal requirements for electric providers to have a 100% clean energy portfolio by 2040 and meet a 60% renewable energy standard by 2035, among other things.

"Energy is a foundation everything else stands on, and for the last two years, that foundation has been cracking," said state Rep. Pauline Wendzel, a Waterville Republican and chair of the House Energy Committee. A leading sponsor of the effort, Wendzel argued in a Tuesday floor speech the mandates have made "our grid less reliable."

* Trump administration extends order to keep Michigan coal plant open

* Michigan Senate votes to override local decisions on wind, solar energy

But chances of addressing that concern this year appear dead on arrival.

Senate Majority Leader Winnie Brinks, immediately following the vote, told Bridge Michigan in a statement that there's "no chance" either bill gets a vote in the Democratic-led Senate.

"It's a bad idea," Brinks, D-Grand Rapids, said when asked about the push to repeal. "They want to take our state backwards."

Tuesday's pair of votes targets sweeping energy reforms that passed along party lines in 2023 as the Democratic trifecta that then controlled state government looked to speed Michigan's transition off fossil fuels.

The centerpiece of the multi-bill package is a requirement for Michigan utilities to derive 100% of their energy from clean sources by 2040.

It's a goal celebrated by climate advocates who argue Michigan must do its part to combat global warming that is dangerously warming the Earth's climate and jeered by Republican lawmakers who've frequently referred to the move as a "government mandate."

"Michigan families deserve an energy policy grounded in reality," state Rep. Pat Outman, a Six Lakes Republican and a leading sponsor of the repeal package, said in a statement following the vote. "Their utility bills should go toward reliable power they can afford, not expensive mandates dreamed up by out-of-touch politicians and special interests."

Climate advocates condemned Tuesday's vote, arguing it ignores mounting evidence about the cost-competitiveness of renewable energy and the risks of continuing to burn fossil fuels.

Leading global scientists have long warned that human fossil fuel consumption is destabilizing Earth's climate, with effects ranging from rising seas and raging wildfires to expanding droughts and disappearing glaciers.

Globally, the past three years have been the three warmest on record. The cost of extreme weather events caused by climate change is expected to reach into the trillions by midcentury.

"This is a really disappointing sign that our House Republicans are stuck in a very old ideological viewpoint that is much more about partisan politics and agreeing with the presidential administration than it is about actual reality," said Denise Keele, executive director of the Michigan Climate Action Network.

Groups representing local governments have taken issue with a provision of the climate package that established statewide standards for large renewable energy projects, allowing the state to permit projects over local objections.

Lawmakers made that change in hopes of speeding up renewable energy developments that were being delayed or blocked amid fierce opposition from rural communities.

But some local government advocates have argued it strips communities of their right to control their own destiny.

Dozens of local governments sued the state in hopes of regaining more control, but an appeals court panel last week left most of Michigan's solar siting regulations in place.

value.Title: Michigan courts uphold state PSC’s permitting authority under new law

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-SD43-RW3M-72C0-00000-00Michigan courts uphold state PSC’s permitting authority under new law2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisMichigan courts uphold state PSC’s permitting authority under new law

The Michigan Court of Appeals ruled in support of key parts of the state’s implementation of the Clean Energy and Jobs Act.

Most importantly, the court upheld the Michigan Public Service Commission’s (MPSC’s) authority to implement the permitting processes established under Public Act 233 (PA 233).

This law ensures that farmers can use their own land for wind and solar generation and energy storage, while requiring these projects to be carefully examined before permission is granted.

Overall, the law was designed to create a statewide, fair siting and permitting process for renewable energy projects, creating a clear, predictable framework with the goal of attracting significant business investment.

The MPSC had argued before the courts in defense of its ability to implement the siting and permitting provisions of Public Act 233. The commission pointed to the clear intent of the legislature and the governor in passing and signing the measure.

The Michigan Energy Innovation Business Council (Michigan EIBC), Institute for Energy Innovation, Clean Grid Alliance, and Advanced Energy United also participated in oral arguments. They all pointed to the importance of PA 233 in providing legal certainty and stability for developers, investors, and communities by having a path to obtaining a permit.

“PA 233 positions Michigan to compete for new energy investment at a time when demand for electricity is rising at historic levels,” Erika Kowall, director of Midwest State Affairs for the American Clean Power Association, said. “A more predictable siting supports faster deployment of new generation, strengthens grid reliability, and helps keep costs low for families and businesses.”

Further, a broad set of business groups, renewable energy advocates, labor organizations, legislators, and local landowners filed amicus curiae brief petitions in support of the commission’s actions to fully implement the permitting process established under PA 233.

“The Court of Appeals affirmed the ability for Michiganders to use their land as they wish while stimulating job creation and economic development,” Dr. Laura Sherman, president of Michigan Energy Innovation Business Council and Institute for Energy Innovation, said. “We expect to remain engaged in this legal process to ensure that Michigan remains a national leader in the clean energy industry, while protecting property rights and supporting local communities.”

The post Michigan courts uphold state PSC’s permitting authority under new law appeared first on Daily Energy Insider.

value.Title: Years after sediment bomb, 5M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sediment

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ7-KN03-SG1V-M152-00000-00Years after sediment bomb, 5M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sediment 2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisAPg. 001Years after sediment bomb, 5M deal ends river damage case Days before trial, Morrow Dam owner settles case over a botched drawdown that smothered the Kalamazoo River with tons of impounded sediment Garret Ellison - gellison@mlive.com

LANSING -- More than six years after it released an estimated 369,000 cubic yards of sediment into the Kalamazoo River during a botched drawdown, a hydroelectric dam company has agreed to pay millions to settle a lawsuit days before trial.

Eagle Creek Renewable Energy will pay 5 million to the state of Michigan following a rapid 2019 drawdown at the Morrow Dam near Kalamazoo, which smothered the river with decades of sediment from Morrow Lake that buried aquatic habitat, altered the river system and complicated an ongoing contamination cleanup.

The company settled April 24, days ahead of a bench trial scheduled to begin April 27 in front of Ingham CountyCircuit Court Judge Morgan Cole, who signed the consent judgment April 30.

The money will go into a state-controlled fund for restoration projects across the Kalamazoo River watershed.

In exchange, the state agreed to drop all claims tied to the 2019 drawdown, including allegations of environmental violations and natural resource damages. The agreement does not require Eagle Creek to perform cleanup work or admit wrongdoing.

Eagle Creek, which was acquired by the Apollo Global Management private equity firm this year, owns and operates Morrow Dam through its subsidiary, STS Hydropower LLC.

Attorney General Dana Nessel's office sued STS and Eagle Creek in 2022 on behalf of the Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes and Energy and Department of Natural Resources.

Nessel's office said the deal "guarantees benefits for the Kalamazoo River and for the public" although the settlement stops short of the full trial remedy the state was seeking.

According to Eagle Creek's trial brief, the state was demanding "nine figure" natural resource damages. The state also sought an order to restore the river and lake, arguing the Kalamazoo River and Morrow Lake were still not fully recovered years later.

"This 5 million dollar settlement to repair the devastation caused by the mismanaged drawdown is long overdue," Nessel said. "Residents deserve to safely enjoy waterways without the threat of manmade hazards. Companies that profit from Michigan's natural resources must be held accountable for the damage they cause."

----

IMPACT WORSENED

The settlement comes roughly 61/2 years after Morrow Dam operators rapidly lowered the 1,000-acre lake by 9 feet starting Oct. 31, 2019, to repair aging spillway gates -- a move state officials say mobilized huge quantities of sediment.

That sediment -- enough to fill 36,900 standard 10-yard dump trucks or about 113 Olympic-sized swimming pools -- was enough to cover the river bottom in one foot of muck for more than 12 miles, according to a Michigan State University analysis.

The damage worsened during spring 2020, when high flows pushed more Morrow Lake sediment downstream at the same time fish were spawning, according to the DNR. Local anglers were aghast as vast, deep mudflats formed along banks and filled pools. River backwaters filled up with fine particles of impoundment sludge.

Upstream of the dam, kayakers got stuck in mudflats and required emergency rescue by airboat. Downstream, people sunk waist-deep in the riverbank and waited hours for help. Kalamazoo Police warned people in 2021 to avoid the deposits, which could be "10 feet deep or more and are like quicksand."

The drawdown was supposed to last four months but dragged on an entire year before the gate fix was completed.

Under pressure from state agencies, Eagle Creek pledged to dredge out the muck, but only ended up removing about 2,000 cubic yards from a side channel near Wenke Park in 2021.

The sediment influx also complicated a federal Superfund cleanup.

The release forced cleanup planners to reconfigure work in the river after new Morrow Lake sediment buried PCB-contaminated areas. In 2022, a Georgia-Pacific spokesman said the sediment "easily added" 0 million to the cleanup, while Environmental Protection Agency project manager Jim Saric said the new material added two to four feet on top of areas the companies already planned to dredge.

Georgia-Pacific intervened in the case in 2023, arguing the Morrow sediment increased the cost and complexity of cleanup.

The company dismissed claims against Eagle Creek and STS with prejudice in March, about a month before the state settlement. The order does not disclose any terms and Georgia-Pacific declined comment.

The fallout from the drawdown also rippled into Lansing, where lawmakers cited the incident as evidence of gaps in Michigan's environmental enforcement laws. In 2024, the Legislature approved changes giving state regulators clearer authority to order emergency action when dams threaten natural resources.

----

AN UNNECESSARY CRISIS'

Regulators said the deluge of mud was avoidable.

State attorneys argued dam maintenance is typically routine, but Eagle Creek "missed every opportunity" to avoid or minimize environmental damage. The dam did not qualify for an unpermitted drawdown, Eagle Creek did not present a plan or consult with the state before the drawdown and it failed to take steps to prevent sediment discharge, the state argued in court.

"Their inaction turned commonplace gate maintenance into an unnecessary crisis that destroyed the river," state attorneys argued.

The state also sought to keep Eagle Creek directly on the hook, not just its subsidiary. In trial briefs, the state called STS a "sacrifice" being offered in Eagle Creek's place and argued the parent company's "direct and permeating involvement" in daily dam operations made it liable regardless of whether the court pierced the corporate veil.

Eagle Creek and STS are located at the same address in Bethesda, Maryland, and share a common president and CEO, Neil Simmons, who signed the April settlement.

Similar corporate liability sheltering has been a point of warning among opponents to the proposed Consumers Energy hydroelectric sale this year to the Hull Street Energy private equity firm, which plans to operate each dam under an individual LLC.

In court, Eagle Creek and STS argued the drawdown was a safety move. The companies claim they had "no feasible or prudent alternative" after learning Morrow Dam was at risk of imminent failure. They said the action protected people and property downstream, and argued the state was trying to punish them for preventing "a disaster like the failure" of Edenville Dam.

The two companies say they operated under Federal Energy Regulatory Commission oversight and relied on federally approved independent consultants, none of whom previously reported the dam was at imminent risk of failure or recommended urgent repairs before October 2019.

In a recent statement, the company maintained its actions were necessary to reduce pressure on deteriorating gates and protect downstream communities.

"The actions we took on October 31, 2019, saved lives and protected property," STS stated.

By contrast, Federal Energy Regulatory Commission spokespeople described the gate work in mid-2020 as intended to reduce stress and mitigate risk but said it was "not considered a dam safety issue."

----

LASTING IMPACT

Despite the passage of time and water, signs of strain remain.

Ryan Baker, a Kalamazoo angler who has tracked conditions along the river for years, said areas near the dam have improved, but lots of sediment remains downstream and clogging backwaters and oxbows.

Many of the mudflats grew vegetation and became permanent fill.

"I can tell you 100% certainty that there's still a bunch up here through downtown (Kalamazoo) and into Plainwell," Baker said. "A lot of it has moved further downstream as well. It's between here and Lake Allegan" about 40 miles downriver.

Matt Diana, a fisheries biologist with the DNR, said the river has partially recovered as sediment has shifted and redistributed.

The main channel has cleared out through much of the Kalamazoo reach and smallmouth bass spawning has seen some recovery. But impacts remain. The state estimated 200,000 freshwater mussels in the river and Morrow Lake died as result of the drawdown.

"A lot of the sediment in the upper river evacuated, especially during some of the high flood flows we've had in the last few years," Diana said. "But we're still seeing it in places, like along the banks -- and in the impoundments that act as sediment traps."

Diana said the river has recovered and improved to some degree.

"We see better substrate exposure and gravel beds and things like that in some of the areas that were covered," he said. "But there's still some places where that sediment sits and holds in place."

"We're hoping to see recovery of the mussel beds through time," Diana said. "Those are long lived and non-mobile species, so they don't recover as quickly as, say, fish that can move in from non-impacted reaches."

Although the 5 million hasn't been attached to any specific restoration projects yet, the settlement language marks it for EGLE and DNR "to restore ecological and natural river functions to streams and rivers within the Kalamazoo River Watershed."

That probably doesn't involve sediment removal.

"It's spread out. It's hard to get at and probably not the best lift," Diana said. "We'll look at larger scale projects to improve the functional lift for the river as a whole" such as "dam removals and large-scale habitat projects."

Morrow Dam owner Eagle Creek Renewable Energy will pay the state of Michigan 5 million in a settlement over a 2019 drawdown that released tons of sediment. MLive.com files

value.Title: In the Skies, Streets and Stories: MTU Enterprise Program Continues to Expand After 26 Years

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-DT53-RSYY-C12D-00000-00In the Skies, Streets and Stories: MTU Enterprise Program Continues to Expand After 26 Years2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisIn the Skies, Streets and Stories: MTU Enterprise Program Continues to Expand After 26 YearsTargeted News ServiceHOUGHTON, Michigan

Michigan Technological University issued the following news release:

* * *

In the Skies, Streets and Stories: MTU Enterprise Program Continues to Expand After 26 Years

By Coby-Dillon English

Rooted in discovery-based learning for 26 years, Michigan Tech's Enterprise Program continues to grow, offering new aerospace, data science and publishing teams with a fresh take on interdisciplinary teamwork to produce measurable results for real clients.

On a campus where hands-on, practical education is the universal standard, Michigan Technological University's Enterprise Program has long pushed the needle of innovative, student-led education. Students collaborate across disciplines on over 25 teams, working on multi-year projects. They design and manufacture real working prototypes, test groundbreaking software and technology, and compete in international and national competitions. Open to all Michigan Tech students, Enterprise supports teams working in aerospace, renewable energy, video game development, robotics, product manufacturing and much more.

Last year, the Enterprise Program celebrated 25 years of excellence and innovation alongside Michigan Tech's Design Expo, the annual showcase event for both Enterprise and Senior Design teams. Continuing forward as a hallmark of the Michigan Tech experience, Enterprise has added three new teams: Aeronautics and Rocketry, Urban Intelligence Lab, and Ink & Ore.

Nagesh Hatti, Enterprise Program director, sees the continued growth as a natural progression as Enterprise continues to respond to both the industry demands and the educational resources students have come to expect.

"The emergence of Enterprise teams in areas like aeronautics, urban intelligence and the humanities highlights the breadth of experiential learning we offer," said Hatti. "Whether students are working on rockets, designing systems that support human interaction in complex environments, or creating literary work, they gain real-world, interdisciplinary skills aligned with today's industries."

"That continued relevance is why the Enterprise Program continues to grow and thrive after 26 years." Nagesh Hatti, director, Enterprise Program.

While the trio of teams are the newest players on the roster, all three are already proving their worth in international competitions and community engagement. Team members are pouring the well-known ingenuity of Michigan Tech Huskies into the hands-on creation of distinctive firsthand learning experiences. For Huskies on all three teams, that means participating in one-of-a-kind opportunities they've helped to build from the ground up.

AERE: A Long Time Coming

While the Aeronautics and Rocketry Enterprise (AERE) is only a year old, it has a much longer history on campus. Members of the Michigan Tech student organizations Keweenaw Rocket Range (KRR) and Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) Aero initially joined forces to help secure more funding for national and international competitions.

During the fall 2024 semester, KRR member Jase Edwards, a junior studying computer science, along with five other students and their faculty advisor Kazuya Tajiri, an associate professor of mechanical and aerospace engineering, met weekly to lay the groundwork of what would eventually become the Enterprise team. AERE was officially founded in spring 2025. Since then, it's grown quickly.

"The idea of AERE has existed since before I was a student at Michigan Tech," said Edwards, AERE founder and program manager. "Even though this is only our third semester, we currently have over 65 members, making us one of the larger Enterprise teams."

With members from a range of degree programs, AERE pushes the boundaries of aerospace innovation. Members are involved in propulsion research, autonomous flight systems, and design and testing for rocketry and remote control aircraft. AERE consists of four teams: the High Powered Rocketry Team, Keweenaw Propulsion Lab, Husky Air and Project Theseus.

During their first semester of operation, AERE's High Powered Rocketry Team collaborated with KRR to compete in the 2025 International Rocket Engineering Competition (IREC), the world's largest rocket engineering competition. They placed in the top third of competitors in their IREC debut and will be competing again this June.

What was once Michigan Tech's SAE Aero club has now become Husky Air. This subteam continues to compete in the annual SAE Aero Design competition, where students design, build, test and fly large remote-controlled airplanes, gaining experience in the full engineering lifecycle. Before AERE's founding, Tech's SAE Aero team was nearly out of commission due to low membership, but Husky Air has continued to grow on the foundations of the former student organization. This year, they competed in the SAE Aero Design Regular Class competition with their 10-foot wingspan aircraft.

AERE's research arm consists of the Keweenaw Propulsion Lab, which is working on an open-source liquid rocket engine with a long term goal of propulsively landing a rocket. Project Theseus, the newest subteam, is developing drones in order to test autonomous flight in complex environments.

Edwards initially joined KRR and eventually AERE to apply their computing acumen in the aerospace field, an industry they hope to break into following graduation. Now, they are gaining new skills as a program manager.

"Last spring, I was chief engineer of the High Powered Rocketry Team," they said. "Now, as program manager, I oversee the entire Enterprise team and manage our operations and communications."

Edwards also feels a sense of responsibility for fellow Enterprise teammates. As AERE continues to grow rapidly, they are committed to keeping the heart of the team focused on experiential education.

"I see Enterprise as a place for students to learn rather than just succeed. I always tell the team leads that my first goal is safety, my second goal is that they learn something and my third goal is success." Jase Edwards, computer science.

Edwards said that while they miss some of the hands-on work that they've had to forego in order to lead the team, they're proud of how far AERE has come. "It feels nice to be in charge of something that has grown this much in such little time," they said. "I feel like a key cornerstone of this team."

Edwards hopes that AERE continues to grow. They're also looking forward to some cross-team collaboration in the future.

"What would be really nice to see would be for us to compete in multiple categories in the International Rocket Engineering Competition, potentially using a rocket engine developed by Keweenaw Propulsion Lab," they said. "It would be nice to see those two teams coming together to launch a completely in-house designed and built rocket."

While that goal may be a while off, Edwards is still thinking about safety first. "I'd want everything to be thoroughly tested before we do that," they said

Urban Intelligence Lab: Tapping Into Local Data

With a growing focus on artificial intelligence (AI) and data science, both in industry and on Michigan Tech's campus, the new Urban Intelligence Lab Enterprise (UI Lab) is giving students the opportunity to connect people, data and cities to make communities more sustainable and resilient. Founded in fall 2025, the UI Lab started its work right here in the Keweenaw.

When data science junior Anna Tolles heard there was going to be a new Enterprise team focused on AI, she was immediately intrigued and reached out to the faculty advisor Gabriel Draughon, assistant teaching professor in the Department of Engineering Fundamentals.

"He sent me a lot of information. I read it all and was really interested!" she said. Tolles ended up being on campus over summer 2025, working with Summer Youth Programs, and had a hand in establishing the new Enterprise team.

"I helped make the website and get things up and running," she said. "That's how I became president, because I was the one up here."

As UI Lab president, Tolles oversees the logistics of the Enterprise team, but still has the opportunity to work on team projects, all of which currently have a local focus.

Last fall, Tolles was working on the campus activity tracking project. Using computer vision from the William J. Bernard Jr. Family Clock Tower webcam stream, the project detects, tracks and maps how students move through campus and interact with its infrastructure. This project allows new UI Lab students to practice some of the skills necessary for other human-centered data projects.

"We made a 2D map out of a 3D space so the camera could understand what was happening," explained Tolles. "We know when we see someone far away that they aren't actually small and getting bigger as they approach, but the camera doesn't know that, so we try to make an aerial view so the camera can understand."

In just one academic year, UI Lab has expanded to new local projects, working with Tech's Great Lakes Research Center to track and classify vessels traveling through the Keweenaw Waterway and collaborating with Mont Ripley ski area to track skier use for potential improvements. Tolles is a member of the Mont Ripley winter sports team, which utilizes cameras in ways similar to the campus activity tracking project.

"With Mont Ripley, we are looking for what paths people use most, how many people are on the paths and in specific areas, which paths are faster than others and so on," said Tolles. "Our goal is to hopefully create a dashboard for the Mont Ripley staff to use to help predict how many people will be on the mountain at this time, based on weather, temperature, day of the week, etc. Down the line, we could be able to include crash detection to automatically flag ski patrol."

Their work has been recognized for its originality and promise, winning first place at this year's Design Expo Awards in the Enterprise Team category recognizing outstanding work.

Tolles said the rewards go beyond prizes to include valuable experience. She's personally gained skills in team building, technical communications and public speaking, and is helping other UI Lab team members do the same.

"Being a part of an Enterprise is really good professional development. That's essentially how I got my internships for this semester and this summer. Having projects under my belt and being able to talk about that work is impressive to hiring managers." Anna Tolles, data science.

Being a new, small Enterprise team has its learning curve, but Tolles is eager to keep moving forward and see the team grow.

"We are a constantly developing Enterprise, so things are changing all the time," she said. "Anyone interested in data science in general would make a good fit, but we have members who study software engineering, civil engineering and even some mechanical and aerospace engineering members. We are open to anyone who is interested."

UI Lab's projects and local focus are expanding right along with membership. The team is currently working with the city of Houghton to use municipal street cameras for a future project. For Tolles, that growth means that UI Lab will continue to get better and better.

"We are running on minimal data right now just because we haven't had a super long time to collect it yet," she said. "I'm excited for us to have more data to work with and create predictive models for the future. The more data you have, the more accurate you will be at predicting things."

Ink & Ore: Telling Student Stories

Branching into new territory for the Enterprise Program, the Ink & Ore Enterprise is a student-run team of publishers, editors and communicators founded in fall 2025. Advised by Holly Hassel, professor of writing studies and director of the First-Year Writing Program, and M. Bartley Seigel, professor of humanities and director of the Michigan Tech Writing Center, the team currently oversees three student publications.

The Ink & Ore team manages the University's 105-year-old student-run newspaper, The Lode, as well as the newer publications Paw Prints: The Michigan Tech Journal of First-Year Writing and the literary magazine Kupari.

By combining both long-standing tradition and adding new publications to the campus culture, students test their writing, editing and communication skills, working directly with their peers. Paw Prints enables students across academic disciplines to publish their coursework as meaningful scholarship, while The Lode continues to foster a journalistic community of practice for the benefit of both student contributors and the campus community.

While The Lode and Paw Prints both predate the establishment of Ink & Ore, the team recently celebrated the debut of Kupari, which published its first issue of short stories, poetry and photographs from Michigan Tech students last month.

The debut issue features works by more than 10 students. As editors Max Meyer, Charles Driscoll and Kupari Lead Devin Palczewski noted in the introduction, "By creating a place to compile the endeavors of our student community, we aim to amplify their voices and inspire further creativity throughout Michigan Tech." In a year-end celebration, contributors and editors gathered to highlight this new kind of publishing experience for Tech students, who gain both useful feedback and bylines.

Open to all students across all majors, Ink & Ore is housed in the Department of Humanities. Continuing the legacy of the Enterprise Program's commitment to career readiness, students gain technical expertise in design, project management and rhetorical communication.

Through their various publications, Ink & Ore team members work directly with student authors to turn their ideas into articulate and thought-provoking articles and stories, all while promoting a richer, student-focused publishing culture on campus.

The successful launch of these three Enterprise teams opens up new avenues for discovery-based learning, helping to shape industry-ready Michigan Tech graduates.

* * *

Michigan Technological University is an R1 public research university founded in 1885 in Houghton, and is home to nearly 7,500 students from more than 60 countries around the world. Consistently ranked among the best universities in the country for return on investment, Michigan's flagship technological university offers more than 185 undergraduate and graduate degree programs in science and technology, engineering, computing, forestry, business, health professions, humanities, mathematics, social sciences, and the arts. The rural campus is situated just miles from Lake Superior in Michigan's Upper Peninsula, offering year-round opportunities for outdoor adventure.

* * *

value.Title: Township preps for legal fight against solar farm Trustees on April 29 approved a 00,000 pot to pay for legal fees to fight the 1,900-acre commercial project.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-P5P3-RTB0-B15S-00000-00Township preps for legal fight against solar farm Trustees on April 29 approved a 00,000 pot to pay for legal fees to fight the 1,900-acre commercial project. 2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisAPg. 002Township preps for legal fight against solar farm Trustees on April 29 approved a 00,000 pot to pay for legal fees to fight the 1,900-acre commercial project. John Tunison - jtunison@mlive.com

As the prospect for a 1,900-acre solar project looms for a rural Ottawa County township, local leaders have targeted an initial 00,000 to help fight the development.

Zeeland Township trustees on April 29 approved the funds.

They expect funds will be needed as RWE, the Germany-based energy company behind the 200 megawatt project, looks to potentially bypass the township permitting process and instead seek state approval.

Public sentiment toward the 00 million development, known as "Silver Maple Solar," has been decidedly negative since information about it first surfaced last summer.

Residents have packed several meetings, raising concerns about water runoff, environmental issues, aesthetics and loss of prime farmland.

RWE and other developers of renewable energy projects have a right to bypass local government approval through Public Act 233 of 2023.

Under that process, the Michigan Public Service Commission would have the final say and not Zeeland Township.

The state process likely will be a lengthy one.

An administrative law judge will look at briefs submitted by both sides, followed by an in-person hearing to question statements from experts.

The next step in the state process is a June 4 "pre-hearing" conference.

As part of the April 29 meeting, Zeeland Township trustees also passed a resolution to officially oppose the proposed solar farm.

In the resolution, trustees cited the community opposition and the importance of agriculture to the "history, culture and economic vitality" of the township.

As part of requirements to Act 233, RWE has provided 5,000 to the township to help cover costs of participating in the MPSC process.

RWE, while pursuing the state approval route, also intends to continue efforts to obtain a township permit.

Township planning commissioners now are in the process of crafting a renewable energy ordinance to govern projects involving wind, solar and battery storage.

value.Title: IN THE SKIES, STREETS AND STORIES: MTU ENTERPRISE PROGRAM CONTINUES TO EXPAND AFTER 26 YEARS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ8-BM03-RV4B-G3G3-00000-00IN THE SKIES, STREETS AND STORIES: MTU ENTERPRISE PROGRAM CONTINUES TO EXPAND AFTER 26 YEARS2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisIN THE SKIES, STREETS AND STORIES: MTU ENTERPRISE PROGRAM CONTINUES TO EXPAND AFTER 26 YEARSStates News ServiceHoughton, Michigan

The following information was released by Michigan Technological University:

By Coby-Dillon English

Rooted in discovery-based learning for 26 years, Michigan Tech's Enterprise Program continues to grow, offering new aerospace, data science and publishing teams with a fresh take on interdisciplinary teamwork to produce measurable results for real clients.

On a campus where hands-on, practical education is the universal standard, Michigan Technological University's Enterprise Program has long pushed the needle of innovative, student-led education. Students collaborate across disciplines on over 25 teams, working on multi-year projects. They design and manufacture real working prototypes, test groundbreaking software and technology, and compete in international and national competitions. Open to all Michigan Tech students, Enterprise supports teams working in aerospace, renewable energy, video game development, robotics, product manufacturing and much more.

Last year, the Enterprise Program celebrated 25 years of excellence and innovation alongside Michigan Tech's Design Expo, the annual showcase event for both Enterprise and Senior Design teams. Continuing forward as a hallmark of the Michigan Tech experience, Enterprise has added three new teams: Aeronautics and Rocketry, Urban Intelligence Lab, and Ink and Ore.

Nagesh Hatti, Enterprise Program director, sees the continued growth as a natural progression as Enterprise continues to respond to both the industry demands and the educational resources students have come to expect.

"The emergence of Enterprise teams in areas like aeronautics, urban intelligence and the humanities highlights the breadth of experiential learning we offer," said Hatti. "Whether students are working on rockets, designing systems that support human interaction in complex environments, or creating literary work, they gain real-world, interdisciplinary skills aligned with today's industries."

"That continued relevance is why the Enterprise Program continues to grow and thrive after 26 years."Nagesh Hatti, director, Enterprise Program

While the trio of teams are the newest players on the roster, all three are already proving their worth in international competitions and community engagement. Team members are pouring the well-known ingenuity of Michigan Tech Huskies into the hands-on creation of distinctive firsthand learning experiences. For Huskies on all three teams, that means participating in one-of-a-kind opportunities they've helped to build from the ground up.

AERE: A Long Time Coming

While the Aeronautics and Rocketry Enterprise (AERE) is only a year old, it has a much longer history on campus. Members of the Michigan Tech student organizations Keweenaw Rocket Range (KRR) and Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) Aero initially joined forces to help secure more funding for national and international competitions.

During the fall 2024 semester, KRR member Jase Edwards, a junior studying computer science, along with five other students and their faculty advisor Kazuya Tajiri, an associate professor of mechanical and aerospace engineering, met weekly to lay the groundwork of what would eventually become the Enterprise team. AERE was officially founded in spring 2025. Since then, it's grown quickly.
