value.Title: Global Infrastructure Projects Roundup

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGP-9CT3-RTMJ-F01T-00000-00Global Infrastructure Projects Roundup2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisGlobal Infrastructure Projects Roundup

This week’s round-up covers a range of project updates, including a USD16.0bn Oracle AI data centre campus in Michigan delivering over 1.0GW of capacity, a USD10.8bn SR 400 Express Lanes scheme in Atlanta adding managed lanes and major corridor upgrades, and Kazakhstan’s USD1.2bn Mirny project combining 1.0GW of onshore wind with 600.0MWh of battery storage.

This week’s round-up covers a range of project updates, including a USD16.0bn Oracle AI data centre campus in Michigan delivering over 1.0GW of capacity, a USD10.8bn SR 400 Express Lanes scheme in Atlanta adding managed lanes and major corridor upgrades, and Kazakhstan’s USD1.2bn Mirny project combining 1.0GW of onshore wind with 600.0MWh of battery storage.Energy & Utilities Asia-PacificAustralia:

Recurrent Energy, the renewable energy division of Canadian Solar, has secured grid connection approval from the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) for its Sundown Energy Park project in northern New South Wales, Australia. Located in the New England Renewable Energy Zone, the project integrates a 360MW solar facility with a 150MW/600MWh battery storage system. Once operational, the development will meet the electricity needs of 176,000 households per year. The project is a key part of Recurrent's 23GW solar and 75GWh battery storage pipeline (PV Magazine).

GenusPlus Group has secured a AUD110.0mn (USD78.5mn) contract for the Koolunga Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) project in South Australia. The 200.0MW/800.0MWh facility will be delivered on a turnkey basis, with scope covering engineering, procurement, construction and commissioning of the balance of plant and battery installation. Located near Koolunga and Brinkworth, the project will connect to the grid via a 1.2km high‑voltage cable to the 275.0kV Brinkworth Substation. Work is scheduled to start shortly, with completion anticipated by September 2027 (Power Technology).

Bangladesh:

Bangladesh Power Development Board (BPDB) has launched tenders for three solar projects totalling 77.6MW in Chittagong, Rangamati and Dinajpur, Bangladesh. The programme includes a 50.0MW plant in Chittagong, a 7.6MW facility at the Karnafuli Hydropower Station in Rangamati and a 20.0MW installation at the Barapukuria coal plant site in Dinajpur. All projects will be developed on a turnkey basis with BPDB and Power Sector Development Fund resources. Completion timelines range from 1 year to 1.5 years, supporting Bangladesh's target of 10.0GW of solar capacity by 2030 (PV Magazine).

Bangladesh Power Development Board (BPDB) has launched a 495.0MW solar tender in Bangladesh. The initiative covers 10 sites with capacities ranging from 25.0MW to 100.0MW, requiring full private investment. Developers must acquire land, arrange financing and propose tariffs, raising concerns over bankability and investor confidence. The tender security is set at USD5,000 per MW. Projects are planned across nine districts, including Kishoreganj, Chattogram and Cox's Bazar, as part of the national goal to reach 10.0GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030. Tender submissions close on June 28 2026 (PV Magazine).

India:

Sterling and Wilson Renewable Energy (SWREL) has secured an 875MW grid-connected solar project at the Pugal solar park from Coal India (CIL) in Rajasthan, India. The contract, valued at INR34.9bn (USD376.8mn), involves SWREL providing design, engineering, procurement, construction (EPC) testing and commissioning services, along with two years of operations and maintenance. Simultaneously, SWREL has received a 50MWac EPC order in Maharashtra, surpassing INR100.6bn (USD1.1bn) in EPC order inflows for 2026. The project is set for completion within two years, with a target CUF of 25% (PV Tech).

Resolven has signed a long‑term power supply agreement with the Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI) for a 180.0MW wind energy project in Andhra Pradesh, India. Executed through its subsidiary Ganeko Three Energy, the project will deliver around 490mn units of clean electricity per year at a tariff of INR3.68 per unit (USD0.044). The output will power approximately 460,000 households and cut nearly 0.4mn tonnes of carbon emissions each year. The initiative strengthens Resolven's wind portfolio, supporting its target of 10.0GWp by 2030 and advancing India's renewable energy transition (Projects Today).

AMPIN Energy Transition has commissioned Odisha's first group captive commercial and industrial solar project in India. The 60.0MWp facility in Kantabanji, developed under the OREP‑22 policy, will supply clean energy to major consumers including Bharti Airtel, Jindal Stainless, Emami Paper Mills and Quality CARE Hospitals. The project reduces conventional grid power use by nearly 50%, delivers 25–30% energy cost savings and cuts carbon emissions by about 77 tonnes per year, equal to planting 3.6 million trees. AMPIN is expanding its portfolio in Odisha to 200.0MW, with 45.0MWp under construction and 50.0MWp in the pipeline (Projects Today).

Japan:

Sumitomo Electric has secured selection for its vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) project in Japan. The 33.0MWh system (11.0MW × 3 hours) will be installed at Hokkaido Electric Power Network's Minami‑Hayakita substation to support wind power grid interconnection. The project, developed with Abira Town's co‑operation, includes a 20‑year service agreement covering operations, maintenance and decommissioning. It forms part of a solicitation process enabling 97,000kW of wind capacity to connect to the grid. Completion is scheduled for May 2029, reinforcing renewable integration and lifecycle sustainability (Energy Global).

Mainland China:

Huaneng has commissioned the Xiaowan Hydropower-Solar Hybrid Project in Yunnan, Mainland China. The facility integrates 4.2GW of hydropower and 1.23GW of solar capacity under a coordinated hydro-solar intelligent control system linking 16 photovoltaic plants. The project leverages the Xiaowan Dam's 15.0bn cu m reservoir to enable multi‑year storage, flood control and stable downstream flow while functioning as a natural battery for renewable integration. Full localisation of core equipment, including monitoring systems and STATCOMs, has been achieved (PV Magazine).

Taiwan, China :

DeepOcean has secured a contract for inter-array cable installation at the 295.0MW TPC-II offshore wind farm in Taiwan, China. The firm will handle the installation of cables connecting turbines to offshore substations, providing engineering and project management services. DeepOcean will collaborate with Dong Fang Offshore, utilising the upgraded vessel Orient Adventurer, equipped with two new ROVs, an under-deck carousel and a cable lay spread. The project, set to start immediately, is expected to be completed in 2026 (reNEWS).

EuropeBulgaria:

Natsionalna Elektricheska Kompania EAD (NEK EAD) has signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) with Toshiba to restart the Chaira pumped‑storage hydropower plant in Bulgaria. The 864.0MW facility, located in the Rhodope Mountains near Devin, has been offline since March 2022 following a turbine failure. Commissioned between 1995 and 2010, the plant operates four reversible Francis pump turbines and plays a critical role in regional energy storage and grid stability. Toshiba will repair Unit I, provide technical studies and support operations across all units to enhance reliability and efficiency. Restoration work is expected to progress from 2026 (PV Magazine).

Cyprus:

The authorities have approved preliminary grid connection terms for eight standalone battery storage projects totalling 231.0MW/570.0MWh in Cyprus. The projects, which will operate without subsidy support, include five 12.0MW/40.0MWh systems by Elestore, a 32.0MW/100.0MWh project by Havvneve Storage, a 59.0MW/120.0MWh installation by H.E.S.S Hybrid Energy Storage Systems and an 80.0MW/160.0MWh project by the Electricity Authority of Cyprus. These early‑mover projects are expected to benefit from significant renewable curtailment on the island. Final approvals are pending, with construction permits already secured for Elestore's systems (PV Magazine).

Finland:

Downing has developed a co‑located wind and energy storage project in Finland. The initiative combines the 30.0MW Konttisuo onshore wind farm, acquired in 2022, with a new 20.0MW battery energy storage system in Southern Ostrobothnia. The project demonstrates Downing's strategy of integrating renewable generation with storage to optimise grid capacity, improve compliance with operator requirements and enhance asset performance (Energy Global).

Germany:

Noveria Energy has finalised a grid connection construction agreement with TenneT for a 250.MW battery energy storage system (BESS) in Niedersachsen, north-west Germany. The project aims to enhance grid stability and support the integration of renewable energy, particularly offshore wind from the North Sea. The development is part of Noveria's broader portfolio, which includes over 3GW of BESS assets. The project is scheduled to connect to the grid in Q1 2028 (Energy Global).

Blue Elephant Energy has secured project financing with Commerzbank for the Schafhöfen solar park in Bavaria, Germany. The 268.0MWp ground‑mounted installation in Mötzing will generate around 300.0GWh per year, with a significant share contracted under a long‑term power purchase agreement to supply Deutsche Bahn's traction network. Commerzbank is acting as sole debt provider for the project, which is scheduled for commissioning by September 2027. The development strengthens renewable energy capacity in Bavaria and represents a major milestone in Blue Elephant Energy's expansion strategy (reNEWS).

Eco Stor has progressed the ECO POWER THREE battery storage project in Saxony‑Anhalt, Germany. The facility will deliver 300.0MW of power with 714.0MWh of capacity, structured into three independently operable blocks for gradual commissioning. Around 300 system units are being delivered and installed by June 2026, with technical commissioning of the first components planned for H2 2026 (PV Magazine).

RP Global has secured bridge financing from BayernLB for the 50.0MWp Harbke photovoltaic (PV) project in Germany. Evergy acted as technical advisor, Chatham provided legal advisory and Watson Farley & Williams supported BayernLB. The Harbke site, developed with MaxSolar, is located on a former lignite mining spoil tip between Saxony‑Anhalt and Lower Saxony. Commissioning is planned for September 2026, when the project will start receiving the EEG feed‑in tariff. Panels are already installed, substation works are under way and RP Global is preparing a 15.0MWp extension once grid reinforcement is complete (Energy Global).

Noveria Energy has signed a grid connection contract for a battery storage project in Germany. The 250.0MW/1.0GWh system will be built in northwestern Lower Saxony, where offshore wind power feeds into the Tennet transmission grid. The agreement secures the connection point and marks a key milestone for the project, which is scheduled to be operational by Q1 2028. Once complete, the facility will contribute to grid stability and renewable integration (PV Magazine).

Gascade has launched pre‑environmental impact assessment surveys for the Hybor pipeline project in Germany. The studies, covering vegetation and wildlife along the 120.0km route from Bobbau in Saxony‑Anhalt to Ruckersdorf, will feed into planning applications scheduled for 2027. The project repurposes existing gas infrastructure to transport hydrogen and is due for completion in 2029. Hybor forms part of Germany's 9,000km core hydrogen network, designed to connect production and import clusters with consuming regions. Public information events are also under way, supporting transparency around the next 150.0km stretch (H2-View).

Ireland:

BnM has inaugurated the Derrinlough Wind Farm, a 126.0MW project representing an investment of over EUR150.0mn (USD175.4mn) in Offaly, Ireland. The facility comprises 21 turbines and is supported by a corporate power purchase agreement (PPA) with Amazon. A EUR12.0mn (USD14.0mn) community benefit fund has been established, with initial commitments of EUR467,000 (USD546,500) to local projects, scholarships and neighbour support. The site also includes 24.0km of amenity access (reNEWS).

Italy:

Shell has inaugurated two agrivoltaic plants totalling 76.4MW in Rovigo, Italy. The projects, developed with the University of Padua, combine energy generation with rotational cultivation of crops such as wheat, soybeans, alfalfa, legumes and vegetables. The Canda plant has 34.3MW capacity across 490,000sq m, while the Loreo Marco Polo Solar II project delivers 42.1MW with 370,000sq m of cultivation. Both sites feature elevated panel structures to allow farm machinery access and biodiversity measures (PV Magazine).

CCE has concluded the sale of a photovoltaic (PV) plant in Italy. The 42.0MW facility in Montalto di Castro, covering 435,000sq m and connected through Terna's substation, was completed in November 2025 with equity and bridge financing. The plant is expected to generate 67.0GWh per year, with 80% secured under a ten‑year power purchase agreement. The transaction aligns with CCE's strategy of developing, building and divesting renewable assets while expanding its portfolio for long‑term operation (PV Magazine).

Kazakhstan:

TotalEnergies has taken a final investment decision for the Mirny onshore wind and battery energy storage project in south-east Kazakhstan. The USD1.2bn initiative combines 1.0GW of wind capacity with a 600.0MWh battery system, expected to generate 100.0TWh of renewable electricity over 25 years. Electricity will be sold under a 25‑year power purchase agreement with the government of Kazakhstan. The project includes 150 turbines and storage supplied by Saft, with TotalEnergies holding 60% and Samruk Energy and KazMunayGas each owning 20%. Full capacity is targeted for 2029 (reNEWS).

Lithuania:

European Energy has signed a long‑term power purchase agreement (PPA) with Mars for the 158.0MW Skuodas wind project in Lithuania. The project is expected to generate around 490.0GWh per year, with bundled guarantees of origin included in the agreement. European Energy estimates the facility will avoid 120,000 tonnes of carbon dioxide emissions per year, supporting Lithuania's renewable energy mix. The PPA is designed to strengthen the project's commercial and financing structure, with commercial operation planned for 2028 (reNEWS).

Spain:

Acuamed has unveiled plans for a 50.0MW photovoltaic self‑consumption project for the Águilas‑Guadalentín desalination plant in Spain. The installation will integrate 86,130 modules of 685.0Wp on 1,632 single axis trackers, supported by 145 inverters and eight transformer stations raising voltage to 20.0kV. Covering 481,437sq m, the plant will connect via a 20.0kV underground line to the desalination facility, ensuring stable energy supply and reducing external dependence. The initiative forms part of the Recovery, Transformation and Resilience Plan, aligned with the National Hydrological Plan and Segura basin objectives for 2022‑2027 (PV Magazine).

Grenergy has signed a 12‑year financial tolling agreement with an international utility rated Investment Grade for the battery storage system of its Escuderos hybrid project in Castilla‑La Mancha, Spain. The deal will start in July 2028, with Grenergy managing operations and trading of the 680.0MWh storage facility. Escuderos also includes a 200.0MW solar photovoltaic plant supported by a daytime power purchase agreement signed with Galp in 2020. Construction of the battery system starts in Q4 2026, with commissioning targeted for 2027 (reNEWS).

UK:

Matrix Renewables has closed a GBP245.0mn (USD330.0mn) non‑recourse financing for the Eccles battery energy storage project in UK. The 500.0MW/1.0GWh facility is supported by CIBC, MUFG Bank and NatWest, with NatWest acting as facility agent. Construction started in November 2025 under a partnership with EDF and commercial operations are scheduled for Q3 2027. The project is designed to enhance grid flexibility and energy security, serving around 270,000 households and avoiding 170,000 tonnes of carbon dioxide emissions per year (reNEWS).

Latin America and the CaribbeanArgentina:

The Arauco I solar park has reached the mechanical completion of its photovoltaic system in La Rioja, Argentina. The project, having installed 89,088 modules across 1,536 Trina Tracker solar trackers, has a total peak installed capacity of 64MWp. The milestone marks the successful completion of three construction stages: post-driving, tracker assembly and module installation. With 19,968 piles set as the structural foundation, the assembly was completed in just three months. The solar installation is part of the broader Arauco complex, which combines solar and wind power for improved generation capacity. Further steps include testing, energisation and commissioning (PV Magazine).

Chile:

Sonnedix has signed three power purchase agreements (PPAs) with Copec EMOAC for the Sonnedix Librillo project in Chile. The initiative involves an 117.0MW standalone battery energy storage system (BESS) with a five‑hour capacity, equal to 643.8MWh per day. The agreements will enable Copec EMOAC to integrate night-time energy into its portfolio, while supporting Chile's decarbonisation goals and improving renewable energy integration. The project will supply at the Francisco node in northern Chile, enhancing grid stability and flexibility. Commercial operation is expected in April 2027, consolidating Sonnedix's role in hybrid and storage solutions (Energy Global).

Limes Renewable Energy has obtained environmental approval for the 150.0MW battery energy storage system (BESS) Ganso project in Valparaiso region of Chile. The facility, designed with a five‑hour duration, received its permit through the Relevance Consultation mechanism, which reduces processing times and costs for projects with limited impacts. Positioned in a corridor with growing data centre demand, the project strengthens renewable integration and supports continuous electricity supply. With this approval, Limes now holds three permitted projects in Chile, adding to its portfolio of 180.0MWp solar and 620.0MW storage (PV Magazine).

Korkia has received authorisation for the Terrazas sectioning substation in Chile. The facility will connect the Terrazas hybrid project in the Coquimbo region, integrating 280.0MW of solar generation with 1,240MWh of battery storage. The authorisation covers construction of a 220.0kV substation near Ovalle, designed with capacity for future expansions and open access. The project aims to supply electricity for around 150,000 homes per year, shifting energy to peak demand periods and supporting mining operations in northern Chile. Operations are expected to start by Q4 2027 (PV Magazine).

Mexico:

Arce Energy Development and Solutions has unveiled the Haab Kiin Solar Plant project in Mexico. The 155.59MW facility, located in Uman and Hunucma in Yucatán, covers 3.0sq km and is under environmental evaluation by Semarnat. The design includes 220,698 photovoltaic modules of 705.0W each, mounted on solar trackers and 945 inverters. The total investment is USD134.79bn, with USD40.43bn allocated to site preparation, USD80.87bn to construction and USD2.8bn per year for operation and maintenance. Mitigation measures are budgeted at USD2.69bn (PV Magazine).

Peru:

Zelestra has obtained a permanent easement for the transmission line of its 242.0MWdc Babilonia solar project in Arequipa, Peru. The approval covers a 7.66km, 220.0kV line connecting S.E. Babilonia with S.E. San José, along with easement areas for plant expansion. Babilonia secured USD176.0mn in green financing in 2026 and signed a long‑term power purchase agreement with Celepsa to supply 450.0GWh per year. Once operational, the plant will power about 350,000 homes and create over 500 jobs during construction. Completion is scheduled for 2027 as part of Zelestra's USD1.0bn investment plan in Peru (PV Magazine).

Middle East and North AfricaKuwait:

United Gulf Construction has secured a KWD42.3mn (USD137.0mn) contract for a new water distribution complex in Kuwait. Awarded by the Ministry of Electricity, Water & Renewable Energy, the project covers construction, completion, maintenance and development of the Hawalli facility and associated works. The initiative supports Kuwait's plan to build an integrated water distribution system to meet rising demand, improve operational efficiency and strengthen the resilience of the national network. The project aligns with broader efforts to upgrade critical infrastructure and ensure reliable water services across the country (Zawya).

Kuwait Authority for Partnership Projects and the Ministry of Electricity, Water and Renewable Energy have extended the bid deadline for the Al Khairan Phase I Independent Water and Power Producer (IWPP) to May 31 2026 in Kuwait. The project comprises a 1.8GW power plant and a 125.0mn imperial gallons per day (MIGD) desalination facility. In parallel, the Central Agency for Public Tenders has approved a USD370.75mn contract for the 60 MIGD Doha Seawater Desalination Plant , Phase II, pending final audit clearance (Zawya).

Saudi Arabia:

The Saudi Power Procurement firm (SPPC) has launched the qualification process for six battery energy storage (BESS) projects in Saudi Arabia. Each project will deliver 500.0MW/2.0GWh under a build‑own‑operate model, with winning consortia holding full equity in special purpose vehicles that will sign Storage Services Agreements with SPPC. The tender covers sites in Qassim, Makkah, Madinah and the Eastern Province, with submissions due by May 5 2026. Driven by Vision 2030, the kingdom targets 48.0GWh of storage by 2030, following the grid connection of a 7.8GWh project in December 2025 (PV Magazine).

Tunisia:

AEOLUS has commenced commercial operation of two solar power plants with a combined capacity of 100.0MW in Tunisia. Located in Sidi Bouzid and Tozeur, the facilities each generate 50.0MW and supply electricity to the national utility under a 30‑year power purchase agreement, serving around 120,000 households per year. Developed at a cost of EUR79.0mn (USD92.0mn), the projects secured non‑recourse financing from EBRD and Proparco, with political risk insurance from the World Bank Group. Commissioning took place in January and March 2026, with Scatec managing operations (Power Technology).

North AmericaCanada:

Officials have approved Hydro One Networks to develop the 230.0kV Red Lake Transmission Line in north‑western Ontario, Canada. The 162.0km double‑circuit line will run from the Dryden Transformer Station to the Red Lake Switching Station via Ear Falls, increasing regional electricity capacity by about 400MW, nearly four times the current level. Hydro One will implement its equity partnership model, enabling First Nations to invest up to 50% in the project. Completion is expected in the early 2030s, supporting mining development, grid stability and economic growth in the region (Power Technology).

US:

Commonwealth Fusion Systems (CFS) has progressed on building the world's first commercial fusion power plant in Virginia, US. The firm, which has raised over USD3bn in private funding, aims to generate power in the early 2030s. The planned 400MW plant will be based on fusion technology, different from nuclear fission, and will create clean, low-waste energy. CFS is also evaluating additional sites across the US, including in the eastern Rust Belt. The firm's demonstration reactor in Massachusetts is over 75% complete and will activate in 2027. Construction is scheduled to commence in 2027 (Construction Review).

BrightNight has acquired a 6GW Hybrid development portfolio previously held in its joint venture (JV) with Cordelio Power in US. This acquisition gives BrightNight full ownership, enabling the firm to independently advance, finance and operate these large-scale projects. The portfolio strengthens BrightNight's presence in key US energy markets, including Arizona, Oregon, Washington, and California. The JV, established in 2020, developed utility-scale projects totalling 15.0GW, with 1.1GW already operational or under construction. BrightNight also secured a USD850.0mn credit facility to further its expansion in the western US (PV Tech).

Kindle Energy has started construction of the Wolf Summit Energy project in Harrison County, US. The USD1.2bn facility will be a 600.0MW combined‑cycle gas turbine plant developed with Blackstone Energy Transition Partners. The project is fully contracted to supply power to Old Dominion Electric Cooperative, serving about 1.5mn residents across Virginia, Delaware and Maryland. Construction is expected to generate 500 local jobs and contribute to regional economic activity. The plant will use GE Vernova technology and aligns with West Virginia's 50 by 50 initiative to expand energy output to 50.0GW by 2050 (Power Technology).

CleanCapital has sold the 120.0MW Nottingham Solar project to Energix Renewables in Ohio, US. Located on a former coal strip mine in Harrison County, the brownfield development has been under way since February 2026, with land clearing, access roads and erosion control measures in progress. Energix plans to energise the project by January 1 2027, using US‑made equipment and focusing on community impact and environmental responsibility. The transaction allows CleanCapital to concentrate on scaling its distributed generation portfolio, while Energix expands its US utility scale solar platform, adding to its 6.5GW pipeline (PV Magazine).

The officials have initiated contracts for the 806.0MW Vineyard Wind project in US. The 20‑year power purchase agreements (PPAs) are expected to save ratepayers USD1.4bn over their lifetime. The 62‑turbine array has already supplied power to New England's wholesale market during the past winter, supporting regional energy needs. The activation underscores the role of long‑term contracts in securing customer savings and project stability. Meanwhile, Vineyard Wind remains engaged in a legal dispute with turbine supplier GE Vernova, which has been ordered to continue operations and maintenance work (reNEWS).

Sub-Saharan AfricaBotswana:

Officials have broken ground on the Maun solar‑plus‑storage project in north-west Botswana. Developed by Okavango Solar, wholly owned by Oman's NAQAA Sustainable Energy, the facility will combine 500.0MW of solar capacity with a 500.0MWh battery system under a 30‑year power purchase agreement with Botswana Power Corporation. The project is structured under an independent power producer model and will be owned and operated by Okavango Solar. Once completed, it will become one of Southern Africa's largest solar‑plus‑storage projects, supporting Botswana's target of 50% renewables in its energy mix by 2030 (PV Magazine).

Kenya:

The officials have advanced the planned Dongo Kundu Liquefied natural gas (LNG) Power Plant in Mombasa, Kenya. The USD2.0bn project targets 1.2GW of generation capacity, with an initial 300.0MW phase before 2027 and 600.0MW by 2028 to stabilise the national grid. The National Treasury's Private Public Partnership unit is conducting viability tests covering technical, economic and environmental aspects. The initiative includes LNG import facilities, a 600.0km pipeline from Tanzania and a 30,000 tonne LPG facility, aiming to reduce reliance on diesel plants and lower power costs by 2030. Completion is expected by 2028 (Construction Review).

Zambia:

The officials have launched the Leopard's Hill solar-plus-storage development in a move to stabilise its national grid in Zambia. Developed by Globeleq and co-developer Leopard Investment, the project will combine 250MWp of solar photovoltaic with a 150MW/600MWh battery energy storage system, enhancing grid integration, peak demand mitigation and frequency regulation. The project, valued at USD315mn, aims for a financial close by Q4 2026. The project's Environmental Impact Statement has been approved and construction is expected to start in 2026 or 2027 (Energy Storage News).

Transport Asia-PacificAustralia:

The federal and state governments have advanced construction on a 22.0km stretch of the Bruce Highway in Queensland, Australia. The works, part of the AUD9.0bn (USD6.4bn) Bruce Highway Targeted Safety Program and the AUD1.0bn (USD717.4mn) Safety Package, include road widening, pavement strengthening, drainage improvements and intersection upgrades to improve safety and resilience. The project is supported by AUD7.2bn (USD5.1bn) in federal funding and AUD1.8bn (USD1.3bn) from the state government. Completion of this section is expected by 2027, alongside other upgrades between Rockhampton and St Lawrence (Roads Online).

Tasmania's Department of Infrastructure has advanced stage II of the Northern Roads Package with AUD420.0mn (USD300.0mn) upgrades to the Bass Highway and adjoining corridors in Australia. Jointly funded with AUD366.0mn (USD262.3mn) from the federal government and AUD84.0mn (USD60.2mn) from the state government, the programme covers improvements on the East Tamar, West Tamar and Bass Highways. Works include lane duplication, intersection upgrades and pedestrian and cycling infrastructure to support heavy vehicle traffic and enhance safety. Construction is already under way on the West Tamar Highway, with further upgrades scheduled across the northern corridor (Roads Online).

The officials have announced a AUD1.04bn (USD740.2mn) investment for roadworks in Australia. The programme will rebuild and repair arterial roads, maintain bridges and traffic lights, deliver emergency works, replace signs and manage roadside vegetation. Regional Victoria will receive 70% of the funds. An additional AUD36.9mn (USD26.2mn) will target maintenance on the state's 10 busiest freeways, including Monash, Princes West, Western, West Gate, Hume, Calder, Western Ring Road, South Gippsland, Mornington Peninsula and Tullamarine. Crews are already active, with completion of 187,000 pothole repairs and other works reported in 2025 (Roads Online).

India:

The state government has planned to upgrade 14 state highways in Rajasthan, India. The project, spanning 926km, under the Rajasthan Highways Modernisation Project, is supported by a INR20.3bn (USD214.8mn) loan from the World Bank. The initiative focuses on improving road infrastructure, transport systems and road safety through a mix of EPC and hybrid annuity model (HAM) packages. Key project features include intelligent transport systems, trauma care centres, EV charging stations and measures for traffic decongestion in major cities like Jaipur and Jodhpur (Projects Today).

East Coast Railway (ECoR) has approved a INR3.0bn (USD31.7mn) multi‑phase redevelopment of Brahmapur Railway Station in Odisha, India. The plan includes capacity expansion through the addition of three new railway lines and four platforms under the 3rd and 4th line project, alongside a new station building, yard modifications and relocation of service facilities. A modern concourse with passenger amenities and a 1,098sq m roof plaza for leisure and retail will be developed. The upgrade aims to ease congestion, improve efficiency and enhance passenger experience while supporting future rail growth (Projects Today).

National Highways Authority of India (NHAI) has accelerated construction of the Patna-Bettiah four‑lane expressway in Bihar, with 15% progress achieved in the first two packages between Bakarpur and Manikpur, India. The INR59.96bn (USD632.7mn) project spans 163.68km and will reduce travel time between Patna and Bettiah from six hours to two and a half hours. Key infrastructure includes three major bridges over the Gandak river, 10 flyovers, grade separators and 108 underpasses. Completion is scheduled for 2027 (Projects Today).

Indian Railways has announced progress on the high‑speed rail corridor connecting Amaravati and Hyderabad in India. The 57.0km line from Errupalem to Namburu, including a 3.0km bridge over the Krishna River, will reduce travel time between the two cities to 70 minutes. The corridor will also link Amaravati with Chennai, Bengaluru, Pune and Mumbai, with proposed travel times of 1 hour 55 minutes from Hyderabad to Pune, 48 minutes from Pune to Mumbai and 73 minutes from Chennai to Bengaluru. Completion is expected in 2027 as part of India's broader bullet train initiative (Projects Today).

Mainland China:

China Railway Fourth Bureau has completed and opened the Nanjing-Ma'anshan commuter line in eastern Mainland China. The 54.2km route serves 16 stations, linking Nanjing Metro Line VII at Xishanqiao with Ma'anshan Taibai station. Operated with four‑car EMUs at speeds up to 120.0km/h, the line has capacity for six‑car trains in the future. Construction started in 2021, with depots located in Binjiang and Dangtu'nan. As China's first inter‑provincial commuter line, it connects Jiangsu and Anhui provinces, with fares set at CNY9.0 (USD1.32) for the full journey (IRJ).

Thailand:

The authorities have revived the Landbridge Project in southern Thailand. The initiative involves constructing a 90.0km railway to connect new deepwater ports in Ranong and Chumphon, creating an alternative route to the Strait of Malacca. With an estimated investment of USD31.0bn, the project aims to reduce ocean transit times by four days and cut transport costs by 15%. The Thai cabinet is expected to approve enabling legislation later in 2026, with construction scheduled for completion by 2039 and breakeven forecast within 24 years (IRJ).

EuropeCzech Republic:

The authorities have invested CZK1.5bn (USD71.4mn) in 2026 in its high-speed rail network, focusing on the Dresden-Prague-Brno-Ostrava corridor, in the Czech Republic. The initiative also focuses on international connections to Germany, Poland, Slovakia and Austria. Of this, CZK960mn (USD46.02mn) will come from domestic sources, with CZK530.0mn (USD25.4mn) from European funds. The priority is on high-speed connections, including routes to Germany, Austria and Slovakia, with some secondary projects, such as RS 42 and RS 5 lines, entering a slowdown phase. The core network is targeted for completion by 2040 (Railway Pro).

Netherlands, Germany:

ProRail has initiated construction of the Netherlands-Germany rail link connecting Coevorden in Drenthe to Bad Bentheim in Lower Saxony, Germany. The project includes expansion of Coevorden Station with a new platform, installation of German rail safety systems and upgrades to switches, tracks and signals to improve efficiency. Operated by Bentheimer Eisenbahn, the line will support both passenger and freight services, enhancing reliability on the Vechtdallijnen regional route. Passenger trains will resume cross‑border service for the first time in 87 years, with operations scheduled to start in December 2026 (Railway Pro).

Poland:

Port Polska has launched a tender for the construction of a 14.3km section of the Warsaw-Lodz high‑speed rail line in Poland. The project, part of Line No. 85 within the 'Y' corridor connecting Warsaw, Lodz, Poznan and Wroclaw, will be built for speeds up to 350.0km/h under a design‑build model. Plans include 55 infrastructure works such as bridges, viaducts and crossings, along with 30.0km of road construction or modernisation. Up to five bidders will be shortlisted, with the Warsaw-Lodz section scheduled for completion in 2032 and the full corridor by 2035 (Railway Pro).

Spain:

European Investment Bank (EIB) and the Catalan government have signed a EUR300.0mn (USD351.7mn) loan agreement for the extension of FGC's Line VIII in Barcelona, Spain. The project involves a 4.0km underground tunnel between Placa d'Espanya and Gracia, new stations at Hospital Clinic and Francesc Macia and upgrades to existing infrastructure. The extension will connect the Llobregat-Anoia network directly to central Barcelona, improving integration with metro and tram services. Construction started in 2023, with the project expected to benefit up to 24mn passengers per year through reduced travel times and better accessibility (Railway Pro).

Ministry of Transport and Sustainable Mobility has advanced the Mediterranean Corridor project in Spain. With EUR2.9bn (USD3.3bn) invested between January 2024 and March 2026, the corridor spans 1,838km along Spain's Mediterranean coast, with 870.0km currently under construction or in progress. Development has raised the share of completed or active sections from 45% in 2018 to 83% today. Planned actions for 2026 include freight terminals, port connections and bypasses across Catalonia, Valencia, Murcia and Andalusia, supporting high‑speed rail links among major coastal cities (Railway Pro).

Latin America and the CaribbeanChile:

The Regional Highway Directorate of La Araucanía and the National Highway Laboratory have developed an innovation project for Route S‑61 in Chile. The initiative addresses accelerated wear caused by heavy traffic exceeding 1,500 vehicles per day, proposing a recycled and stabilised granular base with a new tread layer. This approach reduces material consumption, lowers costs to USD379,290 per kilometre compared to USD730,692 for traditional methods and remains within budget limits. The project will serve as a pilot for sustainable rural road conservation, supporting future updates to the Highway Manual and regional infrastructure development (Carreteras Pan-Americanas).

MOP has reported progress on Route 24 improvement works in Chile. The project, located in the Montecristo slope sector of Antofagasta, has reached 30% completion. The Highway Directorate is redesigning the slope geometry and constructing high‑capacity drainage systems to address seasonal rains. The initiative involves an investment exceeding USD48.0mn and aims to enhance connectivity and employment in the region. Delivery is expected in 2027, complementing other strategic infrastructure projects such as Calama's northern access and the beltway (Carreteras Pan-Americanas).

Colombia:

The National Infrastructure Agency (ANI) and Rutas del Valle Concession have inaugurated Bicentennial Avenue in Santiago de Cali, Colombia. The 15.7km single carriageway forms part of Functional Unit III of the 5G Nueva Malla Vial del Valle project, extending Ciudad de Cali Avenue to Jamundí. The works include three vehicular bridges and will ease congestion on the Pan‑American Avenue, benefiting over 39,000 vehicles per day. The wider programme covers 310.0km of road construction, rehabilitation and upgrades, with USD674.0mn in Capex and USD730.0mn in Opex. Operation of the corridor is scheduled until 2050 (Carreteras Pan-Americanas).

The National Institute of Roads (Invías) has advanced the Los Curos-Malaga road corridor project in Santander, Colombia. The organisation has allocated USD105.0mn and plans an additional USD19.3mn investment during 2026. Progress includes paving 80.0km of the 120.0km route, reconstruction of bridges and installation of modular structures to improve mobility. Technical inspections covered multiple strategic points along the corridor, reviewing paving, signage, closures and traffic management. Invías has also engaged with authorities, control agencies and communities to strengthen monitoring and ensure compliance with commitments (Carreteras Pan-Americanas).

Mexico:

The Ministry of Infrastructure, Communications and Transportation (SICT) has advanced the Glorieta de las Mujeres Libres project in Baja California Sur, Mexico. The 1.5km development includes an underpass with two lanes in each direction, bicycle paths, pedestrian crossings and 24,000sq m of green areas. With a total investment of over USD40.0mn, of which USD18.0mn was executed in 2025, the project has generated around 2,100 jobs and is 87.69% complete. Completion is expected by Q2-Q3 2026 (Carreteras Pan-Americanas).

The Ministry of Infrastructure, Communications and Transportation (SICT) has reported progress on the Amado Nervo bridge project in Mexico. The 2.0km crossing, connecting Nayarit with Jalisco, has reached 69.86% completion with an investment of USD53.5mn. Designed with two lanes in each direction, the bridge will improve traffic flow to Federal Highway 200 and State Highway 544, saving 25 minutes on journeys between Riviera Nayarit and Puerto Vallarta. Construction started in July 2025 and is expected to finish in July 2026, generating 2,700 jobs and accommodating 20,000-21,000 vehicles per day (Carreteras Pan-Americanas).

Peru:

The Ministry of Transport and Communications (MTC) has reported progress on the Chinchaysullo overpass and San Clemente pedestrian bridge in Ica, Peru. The overpass is 19.76% complete, while the pedestrian bridge is 27.57% complete with more than USD2.0mn allocated. Both works form part of a USD208.0mn package of 21 projects along the Pucusana-Ica route, managed by COVIPERÚ under Addendum No. XI to the South Pan‑American Highway concession. Completion will benefit over 240,000 residents and improve safety and traffic efficiency (Carreteras Pan-Americanas).

Middle East and North AfricaOman, UAE:

Hafeet Rail, a joint venture between Etihad Rail, Oman Rail and Mubadala Investment, has reached 40% completion on the USD2.5bn rail connection project between Oman and the UAE. Spanning 238.0km, the project includes bridges, tunnels and flood protection systems across diverse terrains. Key works completed include 27mn cu m of earthworks and 100,000 cu m of concrete. Additionally, the project supports future passenger services alongside freight connectivity, enhancing tourism and business travel. Key facilities in Sohar and Al Buraimi are being developed by Larsen & Toubro and Power China (Zawya).

UAE:

The officials have approved the 42km Gold Line Metro project, a fully underground network connecting 18 stations, with a total investment of AED34bn (USD9.3bn) in Dubai, UAE. This significant initiative will integrate with existing metro lines and Etihad Rail, linking Dubai to the UAE's national transportation network. The project further reinforces Dubai's commitment to sustainable transport solutions. The Gold Line represents Dubai's largest infrastructure project to date in the transport sector. The development is set to roll out in 2032 (TradeArabia).

North AmericaUS:

The USD6bn Frederick Douglass Tunnel Project has progressed to replace the 150-year-old Baltimore & Potomac Tunnel, a critical segment in the Northeast Corridor, while maintaining uninterrupted train service in US. The project introduces two parallel tunnels for electric trains, increasing speeds to 160km/h and improving efficiency, safety and capacity. Spanning 16km, it also includes bridge replacements, track realignments and a new accessible MARC station in West Baltimore. The project, funded primarily through the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, aims for completion by 2035, eliminating a major bottleneck for 14mn passengers per year (Construction Review).

ACS Infra, FlatironDragados, Acciona and Meridiam have launched the SR 400 Express Lanes project in Atlanta, US. The 25.75km managed lanes development will expand capacity between North Springs Metropolitan Atlanta Rapid Transit Authority Station in Fulton County and McFarland Parkway in Forsyth County. Delivered through the SR 400 Peach Partners consortium, the scheme includes dynamically priced express lanes, interchange upgrades, bridges and corridor‑wide traffic management systems. The public‑private partnership involves USD10.8bn investment, combining federal credit assistance, private activity bonds and equity. Construction will take 5.5 years, followed by 50 years of toll operations (WCN).

Officials have announced a USD4.7bn investment package for rail projects along the Northeast Corridor, the busiest rail corridor in US. The programme focusses on modernising station infrastructure, streamlining rail services and rebuilding bridges, with priority projects at New York Penn Station and Washington Union Station. Funding will be channelled through federal grants for intercity passenger rail, aiming to reduce the investment backlog and expand services. Eligible projects must be submitted by May 5 2026 and included in the 2026 NEC inventory to qualify for support (Railway Pro).

Gateway Development Commission has awarded Package I‑C of the Hudson Tunnel Project to the Traylor-Walsh-Skanska joint venture under a USD1.29bn contract in US. The scope covers excavation of two 2,210m tunnel tubes beneath the Hudson River between Weehawken, New Jersey and Manhattan, along with cross passages, stabilisation near Hudson‑Bergen Light Rail and underground structural support. Six of the project's 10 packages are already complete or under way, advancing the Gateway Program's objective of expanding passenger rail capacity and enabling rehabilitation of the existing North River Tunnel (Construction Review).

Sub-Saharan AfricaGuinea:

Trans‑Guinean Company (CTG) has advanced the Trans‑Guinean Railway project introducing plans for passenger services on the 650.0km line connecting Simandou's iron ore deposits with the new port at Morebaya in Guinea. Ten stations are planned along the route, with up to two trains per day in each direction once infrastructure is complete. Designed to transport 120mn tonnes of iron ore per year, the line will also support other operators and stimulate agriculture. Shareholding includes 15% by the government of Guinea, with 42.5% each held by Simfer Infraco Guinée and WCS Infraco (IRJ).

Tanzania:

Vossloh has secured a EUR30.0mn (USD35.2mn) contract for the supply of switches and fastening systems for a standard gauge railway project in Tanzania. Covering Sections III and IV, the Makutupora-Tabora (294.0km) and Tabora-Isaka (130.0km) segments, the deal includes 130 switch sets and 840,000 fastening ties, manufactured in Sweden and Germany. These sections, built by Yapi Merkezi under Tanzania Railways Corporation, form part of the 2,000km Dar es Salaam-Mwanza corridor, designed to reduce travel times and strengthen regional trade. Completion of the two segments is expected between 2027 and 2028 (Railway Pro).

Standard Chartered has arranged a USD2.2bn loan for the Tanzania Standard Gauge Railway (SGR) project in Tanzania. The financing, supported by export credit agencies, will fund construction of Lots III and IV covering Makutupora, Tabora and Isaka, part of the 1,219km corridor. Earlier phases included a 541.0km section between Dar es Salaam and Dodoma, financed partly by a USD1.46bn loan. The full SGR network will span about 2,561km, linking Dar es Salaam port with inland centres and neighbouring countries. The loan signing is scheduled in Dodoma on April 28 2026 (Construction Review).

Uganda:

Officials have appointed Citibank to arrange funding for the Kampala-Malaba Standard Gauge Railway (SGR) project in Uganda. The EUR2.7bn (USD3.16bn) electrified line will run 272km with 1,435mm gauge, connecting Kampala to the Kenyan border and linking to Kenya's SGR network and the port of Mombasa. A new contract with Turkish contractor Yapi Merkezi was signed in October 2024, with preparatory works already under way using government resources. Full construction depends on securing financing, with completion targeted as part of the country's regional connectivity strategy (IRJ).

Construction Asia-PacificAustralia:

The Queensland Government has completed the Concept Design phase for the Coomera Hospital, marking a significant milestone in the state's Hospital Rescue Plan, in Australia. The USD1.3bn project will feature a 12-storey Clinical Services Building and a seven-storey Ambulatory Care Building, providing 600 beds upon full completion by 2032. The hospital will support critical services, including emergency care, operating theatres, maternity and mental health facilities. Multiplex Constructions is the appointed contractor and 1,000 workers will be on-site during peak construction. Stage I, targeting 400 beds, is set for completion by 2031, with construction ramping up in 2026 (Construction Review).

India:

ReNew has launched construction of a solar manufacturing facility in Anakapalli, India. The INR54.0bn (USD573.1mn) project will establish a 6.0GW ingot and wafer unit at Rambilli, producing critical upstream components for solar module manufacturing. Supported by Andhra Pradesh's Integrated Clean Energy Policy 2024, the initiative is part of ReNew's INR820.0bn (USD8.7bn) investment commitment in the state. The facility is expected to create over 2,100 jobs and be commissioned within 2 years, aligning with India's 2030 target of 500.0GW non‑fossil fuel capacity (Projects Today).

BPTP has announced Skynest, a luxury residential project in Greater Faridabad, India. The development involves an investment of nearly INR11.0bn (USD116.3mn) with an estimated gross development value of INR18.0bn (USD190.4mn). Skynest will feature 325 premium apartments across twin towers rising to 150.0m, offering three‑ and four‑BHK units ranging from 296sq m to 358sq m. The project integrates sustainable building practices and modern amenities, strategically located with strong connectivity to Delhi via NH‑148NA and the Delhi-Mumbai Expressway corridor (Projects Today).

Telangana Cabinet has approved a INR7.0bn (USD73.8mn) sports infrastructure project in Hyderabad, India. The plan will redevelop a stadium complex covering 307,560sq m into a multi‑sport international facility, expanding seating capacity from 20,000 to 50,000 and creating infrastructure for 21 sports disciplines. A total of 48,562sq m will be allocated for commercial development under public‑private partnership (PPP) arrangements to ensure financial sustainability. The project also includes a sports university, upgraded swimming facilities and residential coaching infrastructure (Projects Today).

Malaysia:

PRIVASIA Technology has commenced construction on a MYR569mn (USD143.3mn) data centre in Perak, Malaysia. The facility, in Bagan Datuk, will offer 10.1MW of IT load capacity. The development, part of Malaysia's broader digital economy agenda, aims to decentralise data infrastructure away from saturated hubs like Klang Valley and Johor. The project aligns with the country's strategy to promote economic growth and employment in less-developed areas. PRIVASIA will oversee operations, with scalability for future expansion. The project is set for Phase I completion by Q4 2027 (Construction Review).

South Korea:

SK Hynix has commenced construction on its KRW19trn (USD12.9bn) P&T7 advanced semiconductor packaging facility in Cheongju, South Korea. The facility will specialise in high-bandwidth memory (HBM3E, HBM4) packaging, chip stacking and AI-oriented memory products. Located within the Cheongju Technopolis Industrial Complex, the project aims to address the growing demand for AI-driven computing and high-performance GPUs. P&T7 will be integrated with existing SK Hynix plants, enhancing production efficiency. Completion is scheduled for Q4 2027 (Construction Review).

EuropeGermany:

The European Semiconductor Manufacturing (ESMC) has advanced its EUR10bn (USD11.7bn) semiconductor fabrication plant, transitioning to vertical construction and early systems preparation in 2026, in Dresden, Germany. The initiative follows the groundbreaking in 2024, with core buildings now rising, marking significant progress. The facility will feature advanced technology nodes (28/22nm and 16/12nm) focused on automotive and industrial sectors, with a capacity of 40,000 wafers per month. The project, pivotal for Europe's semiconductor sovereignty, receives a EUR5bn (USD5.8bn) subsidy from the EU and German government. The plant remains on schedule for its 2027 production start (Construction Review).

Netherlands:

Resilicon has secured strategic project status under the EU's Net Zero Industry Act (NZIA) for its planned 13.0GW polysilicon production facility in Groningen, Netherlands. The plant will produce ultra‑pure polysilicon and silane using renewable energy and green raw materials, supporting Europe's supply chain resilience and job creation. The designation provides streamlined permitting and access to financing advice, while aligning with EU efforts to expand domestic solar manufacturing (PV Tech).

Norway:

Kitebrook Infra has advanced the Nordic Data Centre project in Norway. The USD8.0bn programme targets up to 500.0MW of hyperscale capacity through phased, modular construction across multiple sites, leveraging hydropower for renewable operations. The initiative integrates advanced cooling systems suited to cold climates and prioritises fibre connectivity and grid access to ensure efficiency. Backed by experienced investors and developers, the project aligns with Europe's energy transition goals and rising demand for AI‑driven workloads. Construction phases are planned to roll out over several years starting in 2026 (Construction Review).

Romania:

Cordia, a subsidiary of Futureal Group, will invest EUR65mn (USD76.4mn) in the development of the Centropolitan residential project in central Bucharest, Romania. The complex will offer 274 apartments ranging from one-room to four-bedroom units, alongside 3,345sq m of retail space on the ground floor. The 8,179sq m land plot for the project was acquired from local developer Bog'Art Place. Cordia has previously completed the Parcului 20 project, comprising 485 apartments, in northern Bucharest (SeeNews).

UK:

Eric Parry Architects has secured the approval for a GBP750.0mn (USD1.0bn) redevelopment at Warneford Park in Oxford, UK. The project, supported by Oxford Health NHS Foundation Trust, the University of Oxford and a local donor, will deliver a new mental health hospital and medical research centre alongside the conversion of Grade II‑listed buildings into a postgraduate college. The scheme will provide over 60,000sq m of new facilities, including accommodation for 250 students, teaching and research spaces and landscaped gardens. Construction phases will depend on future funding, with hospital operations continuing during the build (WCN).

Latin America and the CaribbeanParaguay:

Atome has reached Final Investment Decision (FID) on its USD665.0mn low‑carbon fertiliser plant in Villeta, Paraguay. Designed to produce 260,000 tonnes per year using green hydrogen‑based processes, the facility is described as the first industrial scale project of its kind globally to achieve FID. Financing includes USD420.0mn in debt from international development finance institutions and USD245.0mn in equity led by Hy24, IFC, KfW DEG, IFDK, Sudameris and Atome. Construction is expected to start shortly, with full production scheduled by October 2029 (PV Magazine).

Middle East and North AfricaEgypt:

Construction has progressed on Mada, worth USD277mn and part of a broader USD2bn development in Egypt. The 15.3mn sq m Mada masterplan, Egypt's largest entertainment city, includes a 2mn sq m residential zone combining housing, commercial, educational, healthcare and leisure facilities. Led by MIDAR and Emaar Misr, this phased urban project aims to cater to East Cairo's growing population while offering modern infrastructure. The development, located along Suez Road, is expected to attract foreign investment (Construction Review).

Qatar:

The construction of Qatar Academy - Sidra in Education City has advanced to nearly 94% completion in Qatar. Covering 74,000sq m, the project includes a two-story building with classrooms, multipurpose halls and administrative offices, designed to accommodate 1,800 students. The academy features 60 classrooms, a theatre, laboratories, sports facilities and more. The project applies the latest digital technologies, including Building Information Modelling and is being implemented according to sustainability standards, targeting a three-star Global Sustainability Assessment System rating. Final delivery is scheduled for H2 2026 (Zawya).

Saudi Arabia:

Jeddah Tower, the world's future tallest building, has achieved a construction milestone, surpassing 100 floors and reaching 400.0m height in Saudi Arabia. Once completed, it will stand over 1km tall. Thornton Tomasetti is providing structural design services for the project while collaborating with Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill Architecture. The tower will feature high-performance designs and advanced materials, reinforcing Jeddah Economic's vision for a groundbreaking architectural landmark (Zawya).

Diriyah Company has awarded the Saudi Arabia Museum of Contemporary Art (SAMoCA) project in Diriyah with a SAR1.84bn (USD490.0mn) construction contract to a joint venture between Albawani and Hassan Allam Construction in Saudi Arabia. Covering 45,252sq m of gross floor area, the museum will showcase Saudi contemporary art under the Museums Commission and has achieved Mostadam Gold certification for sustainability. SAMoCA will complement the JAX Centre in Riyadh and anchor Diriyah's cultural development, part of the USD63.2bn master plan forecast to contribute USD18.6bn to GDP and generate over 180,000 jobs (WCN).

AirTrunk has advanced the development of its hyperscale data centre campus in Saudi Arabia. The USD3.0bn project, undertaken with Humain and backed by the Public Investment Fund, will deliver 250.0MW of GPU‑based infrastructure for artificial intelligence (AI) training, inference and cloud services. Supported by USD1.2bn financing from the National Infrastructure Fund, the initiative is positioned to attract institutional investors through a potential AI investment platform. Commercial bids from shortlisted contractors are due by May 4 2026 (Construction Review).

UAE:

Arada has unveiled Jenna, a residential complex within the AED35.0bn (USD9.53bn) Aljada megacommunity in Sharjah, UAE. Located on West Boulevard, Jenna-I offers 100 apartments now available for purchase, with all 631 homes scheduled for completion by Q1 2029. Jenna is positioned near key amenities including Raffles World Academy, Aljada's sports complex and Madar Mall, supporting Aljada's growth as a mixed‑use community (Zawya).

Samana Developers has broken ground on five residential projects with a combined value of AED1.9bn (USD517.36mn) in Dubai, UAE. Together, the developments will deliver 1,428 units, including Samana Boulevard Heights (541 units), Samana Imperial Garden (344 units), Samana Greenfield I (310 units), Samana Hills South III (147 units) and Rome III (85 units). The projects are spread across Dubai Land Residence Complex, Arjan, Al Warsan IV, Dubai South and Meydan District 11. According to the Dubai Land Department's REST app, completion is scheduled for 2028 (Zawya).

Nakheel has awarded USD953.0mn contracts for the Palm Jebel Ali villa project in the UAE. The agreements cover construction of 544 villas, with Ginco General Contracting building 354 units on Fronds A to D and United Engineering Construction delivering 190 units on Fronds E and F. The villas feature ten architectural designs with sustainable elements and smart‑home technology, forming part of Dubai's southern coastline expansion. The initiative aligns with the Dubai 2040 Urban Master Plan and Economic Agenda D33. Construction starts in Q2 2026, with completion scheduled for Q4 2028 (WCN).

North AmericaUS:

The officials have launched construction of Building D within the Arverne East development in New York, US. The USD278.0mn project, in Far Rockaway, will deliver 89 cooperative ownership homes and 229 affordable rental apartments, with units designed for households earning up to 100% and 90% of Area Median Income. The building incorporates accessibility features, youth housing with support services and community amenities. Sustainability measures include all‑electric operations, rooftop solar, Passive House standards and integration with a geothermal loop. Commissioning is scheduled for September 2027, contributing to the wider plan of 1,650 homes (WCN).

Beale Infrastructure has launched Project Blue, a hyperscale data centre development in Pima County, Arizona, US. The 1.17mn sq m site will host up to 10 buildings totalling 185,806sq m with an initial phase of four facilities. Representing a USD3.6bn investment, the project will create over 3,000 construction jobs through 2028 and 180 permanent roles with average salaries of USD64,000. Forecasts estimate USD250.0mn in tax revenue over 10 years. The campus will use up to 286.0MW of power, fully matched with renewable energy and adopt air‑cooled systems to minimise water use (Construction Review).

Oracle has secured full financing for the artificial intelligence (AI) data centre campus in Saline Township, Michigan, US. The USD16.0bn project, part of the Stargate initiative, will deliver more than 1.0GW of power capacity across three single‑storey buildings totalling 153,290sq m. Funding includes USD2.0bn in equity from Related Digital and Blackstone and USD14.0bn in long‑term debt anchored by PIMCO. The project will create over 2,500 union construction jobs, 450 permanent onsite roles and 1,500 county‑wide jobs. Construction is under way, with phased delivery scheduled for 2027 (Construction Review).

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's (NYSE:DTE) share price is continuing to outperform that of Public Service Enterprise Group Inc. (NYSE:PEG). DTE Energy Company's return over the past month has been -5.1% and Public Service Enterprise Group Inc.'s return has been -7.1% over the same period.

Here is how DTE Energy Company and Public Service Enterprise Group Inc. compare:

Company Name1 Month %3 Month%YTD %1 yr %DTE Energy Company-5.1-1.311.010.5Public Service Enterprise Group Inc.-7.1-9.9-3.12.7

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's (NYSE:DTE) share price is continuing to underperform that of NiSource Inc. (NYSE:NI). DTE Energy Company's return year to date has been 11.3% and NiSource Inc.'s return has been 14.1% over the same period.

Here is how DTE Energy Company and NiSource Inc. compare:

Company Name1 Month %3 Month%YTD %1 yr %DTE Energy Company-3.96.311.38.1NiSource Inc.-0.76.514.122.0

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

value.Title: Michigan courts uphold state PSC's permitting authority under new law

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-5BR3-RXK9-G1G8-00000-00Michigan courts uphold state PSC's permitting authority under new law2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisMichigan courts uphold state PSC's permitting authority under new law

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. Stories from such authors are provided "AS IS," with no warranties, and confer no rights. The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Newstex Authoritative Content is not "read and approved" before it is posted. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

Dave Kovaleski

May 12th, 2026 ( Daily Energy Insider - Delivered by Newstex )

The Michigan Court of Appeals ruled in support of key parts of the state's implementation of the Clean Energy and Jobs Act.

Most importantly, the court upheld the Michigan Public Service Commission's (MPSC's) authority to implement the permitting processes established under Public Act 233 (PA 233).

This law ensures that farmers can use their own land for wind and solar generation and energy storage, while requiring these projects to be carefully examined before permission is granted.

Overall, the law was designed to create a statewide, fair siting and permitting process for renewable energy projects, creating a clear, predictable framework with the goal of attracting significant business investment.

The MPSC had argued before the courts in defense of its ability to implement the siting and permitting provisions of Public Act 233. The commission pointed to the clear intent of the legislature and the governor in passing and signing the measure.

The Michigan Energy Innovation Business Council (Michigan EIBC), Institute for Energy Innovation, Clean Grid Alliance, and Advanced Energy United also participated in oral arguments. They all pointed to the importance of PA 233 in providing legal certainty and stability for developers, investors, and communities by having a path to obtaining a permit.

'PA 233 positions Michigan to compete for new energy investment at a time when demand for electricity is rising at historic levels,' Erika Kowall, director of Midwest State Affairs for the American Clean Power Association, said. 'A more predictable siting supports faster deployment of new generation, strengthens grid reliability, and helps keep costs low for families and businesses.'

Further, a broad set of business groups, renewable energy advocates, labor organizations, legislators, and local landowners filed amicus curiae brief petitions in support of the commission's actions to fully implement the permitting process established under PA 233.

'The Court of Appeals affirmed the ability for Michiganders to use their land as they wish while stimulating job creation and economic development,' Dr. Laura Sherman, president of Michigan Energy Innovation Business Council and Institute for Energy Innovation, said. 'We expect to remain engaged in this legal process to ensure that Michigan remains a national leader in the clean energy industry, while protecting property rights and supporting local communities.'

The post Michigan courts uphold state PSC's permitting authority under new law appeared first on Daily Energy Insider.

Link to the original story.

value.Byline: Kelly House/bridge Michigan The Associated Press
value.Title: Appeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suit

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ1-D7G3-SCD4-W3YR-00000-00Appeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suit2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisINTERNATIONALAppeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suitKelly House/bridge MichiganThe Associated Press

A state appeals panel on Thursday upheld the bulk of Michigan’s regulations limiting local control over renewable energy projects, while rejecting narrow aspects that critics had decried as regulatory overreach.

The three-judge Michigan Court of Appeals panel ruled that state regulators followed proper legal processes when they set rules to carry out a controversial 2023 law that allows the Michigan Public Service Commission to approve large wind, solar and battery projects over local objections.

But judges ruled the commission interpreted certain aspects of that law in ways that improperly limited local power.

It wasn’t immediately clear how the split ruling could affect the multiple renewable energy development proposals currently awaiting state approval. However, state officials seemed to see it as a victory.

“While the Commission continues to review the impact of specific findings of the Court’s decision on cases before us, today’s decision largely affirms the Commission’s approach and allows for continued and timely implementation of the law,” said Matt Helms, a spokesperson for the Public Service Commission.

A lawyer for the dozens of local communities that had sued the state over its solar permitting rules called the ruling a “mixed bag.”

Attorney Michael Homier said he’s pleased with portions of the ruling that favored his clients, but “disappointed the court didn’t apply the same reasoning” to his clients’ other arguments.

The ruling followed a tense yearslong debate about Public Act 233, which passed along party lines in 2023 as Democrats sought to speed up a renewable energy transition that had been slowed in part by fierce local opposition to planned wind and solar arrays.

After the Michigan Public Service Commission wrote rules to carry out the law in 2024, dozens of local communities sued, arguing the rules undermine local control in ways the law never envisioned.

The law allows local governments to retain jurisdiction over renewable energy proposals so long as they enact a so-called “compatible renewable energy ordinance” containing terms no stricter than the new statewide standards governing noise, setbacks and other particulars.

If they don’t have such an ordinance, developers can instead seek approval from the Public Service Commission.

Among other things, the local government groups argued the commission failed to follow proper rulemaking procedure and wrote overly narrow terms for the local ordinances.

In their ruling Thursday, Judges Christopher Murray, Michael Gadola and Michael Kelly rejected those arguments and several others posed by the local governments.

But they sided with the governments on two issues:

1. The law gives local governments 30 days after a meeting with developers to start the local approval process. The state wrote rules that instead set the 30-day clock to start when developers offer to meet. Judges called that a misinterpretation of the law, rejecting state lawyers’ concerns that local governments may now be able to block projects simply by refusing to meet.

2. The justices agreed with the local governments that any county, township, city or village that touches project lands is considered an “affected local unit,” and thus eligible for certain privileges, including a payment from developers. The state had written rules saying only governments with zoning jurisdiction were considered an “affected local unit.”

In a press release, renewable energy advocates celebrated the ruling, with Michigan Energy Innovation Business Council President Laura Sherman saying it “affirmed the ability for Michiganders to use their land as they wish while stimulating job creation and economic development.”

Officials with the Michigan Townships Association, which has been critical of the state permitting system and lobbies on behalf of dozens of townships involved in the lawsuit, did not respond to a request for comment.

___

This story was originally published by Bridge Michigan and distributed through a partnership with The Associated Press.

value.Title: Renewable energy news: Qair, Orsted, Elements Green

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-8H63-RTHK-N06G-00000-00Renewable energy news: Qair, Orsted, Elements Green2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:54:35ZLexisNexisRenewable energy news: Qair, Orsted, Elements GreenNew Delhi


May 08 --

Today’s renewable energy news includes updates from Qair, Orsted, Elements Green, among others.

Qair Advances North Scotland O&M Base for 1GW Ayre Floating Wind Project

Qair is progressing plans for a long-term operations and maintenance base to support its 1GW Ayre floating offshore wind project off east Orkney and north-east Caithness. The initiative is expected to create around 120 local jobs and strengthen regional supply chain opportunities as part of Scotland’s ScotWind programme. Project teams visited key ports including Port of Kirkwall, Gills Bay, Port of Scrabster and Port of Wick to assess infrastructure and logistics capabilities for long-term support operations. Stakeholder discussions also involved regional councils, enterprise agencies and supply chain organisations. The project highlights investment and innovation in floating offshore wind infrastructure, supporting local economic development, skilled employment, energy security, and Scotland’s transition toward large-scale renewable energy generation in the early 2030s.



Orsted Acquires 150MW Salzburg Battery Storage Project in Michigan

Orsted has acquired the 150MW Salzburg battery energy storage system (BESS) project in Michigan from developer ESA Solar Energy, expanding its growing US energy storage portfolio. The project, located in Midland Township, is targeting commercial operations between 2029 and 2030.  The Salzburg project is designed as a standalone 150MW/600MWh battery storage facility capable of delivering peak shaving, load shifting and grid reliability services. The system will connect directly to Michigan’s transmission network through a dedicated substation and is expected to support the state’s clean energy goals, including a 100 percent clean electricity target by 2040.  The acquisition highlights continued investment and innovation in large-scale energy storage infrastructure, strengthening renewable integration, improving grid resilience, and supporting long-term energy security as electricity demand rises across the US.

Elements Green Acquires 300MW Newarthill Battery Storage Project in Scotland

Elements Green has acquired the 300MW Newarthill battery energy storage system (BESS) project in Scotland from Geocore, strengthening its UK energy storage pipeline. The transmission-connected project, located near Motherwell, is planned as a two-hour duration facility with potential expansion to four hours and is targeting energisation by October 2029.  The project received planning consent in February 2025 and will connect through a nearby 275kV substation under a grid offer secured from the UK’s National Energy System Operator. The strategic location around the B6 and B4 transmission boundaries is expected to help reduce network congestion and support greater renewable energy integration across Scotland and the wider UK grid.  The acquisition highlights growing investment and innovation in long-duration battery storage infrastructure, improving grid flexibility, energy security, and system stability as renewable electricity generation continues to expand.

value.Title: Xcel Energy to spend $12 Million to occupy 37,400 square feet of space in Amarillo Texas .

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-Y9P3-S8SM-536V-00000-00Xcel Energy to spend $12 Million to occupy 37,400 square feet of space in Amarillo Texas.2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:47ZLexisNexisXcel Energy to spend $12 Million to occupy 37,400 square feet of space in Amarillo Texas.Craig Etkin

Amarillo, Texas - According to state and local development sources, Xcel Energy plans to invest $12 Million to build out 37,400 square feet of new space in Amarillo. The company plans to occupy the new space at 4701 Parkside Drive in Amarillo, on or about July 1, 2027. According to the company website, As a leading electric and natural gas energy company, we offer a comprehensive portfolio of energy-related products and services to 3.4 million electricity customers and 1.9 million natural gas customers across our eight states: Colorado, Michigan, Minnesota, New Mexico, North Dakota, South Dakota, Texas and Wisconsin. Our workforce of more than 12,000 is rising to the challenge of a dynamic, constantly-changing utility industry. One that requires us to be even more customer focused, forward thinking and productive while remaining committed to meeting our customers fundamental need for safe, reliable, affordable energy. Do you have a passion for renewable energy, like wind or solar? Or care about giving back to the community? Youll be in a position to directly impact our energy future. At Xcel Energy, youll be challenged, respected and rewarded. Youll find an ethical team committed to excellence, safety and environmental stewardship. A dynamic company where youll have meaningful work that makes a difference.

To learn more about Xcel Energy, visit http://www.xcelenergy.com/

Minneapolis, MN55401
